message.content:
Hey, I'm starting a take-home build exercise. Let me give you the full context before we write any code.

Here's the brief:

[# Build Exercise

A two-phase test that mirrors how you'd actually work if you joined. A backend take-home you can put real thought into, then a fast live frontend build on top of what you shipped.

Use Claude Code throughout. We're as interested in how you build as what you build, and we want to see Claude Code in your hands specifically.

---

## Product context (read once, 2 minutes)

We're building a pharmacovigilance (PV) case processing platform. Software that helps pharma companies process adverse event reports called ICSRs (Individual Case Safety Reports). When a patient has a bad reaction to a drug, that case is documented in a regulated format and a human case processor reviews it before submission to regulators.

We use AI to extract structured fields from source documents. A human reviewer then validates those fields, fixes mistakes, and signs off. Cases often get follow-up updates as new information arrives. When that happens, the AI re-extracts and the reviewer needs to see what changed.

You'll build the backend that handles follow-up case merging and queries, the infrastructure to operate it, and then the screen the reviewer uses to validate the merged case.

You do not need any pharmacovigilance knowledge. Treat it as: structured data extracted by AI from a document, which a human reviewer needs to validate, with v1 vs v2 conflict resolution.

---

## Phase 1A: Java backend take-home (~90 min, before the session)

Build a Spring Boot service that powers case review.

### Endpoints

1. `GET /cases/{caseId}` returns the most recent version of a case.
2. `POST /cases/{caseId}/follow-ups` accepts a follow-up payload (same shape as the initial case, with updated or new fields), merges it onto the stored version, and returns the merged case with diff annotations.
3. `POST /queries` creates a reviewer query against a specific field. Payload: `{caseId, fieldPath, question}`.
4. `GET /queries?caseId={id}` lists queries for a case.
5. `GET /health` returns service health for liveness checks.

### Bootstrap

On startup, load `case_v1.json` (attached) into in-memory storage as the initial version of case `PV-2026-0451`. No database needed.

### Merge behavior (the meaningful part)

When a follow-up is POSTed, for each field:

- Same value as stored version: `status: "unchanged"`
- Different value: `status: "overridden"`, include `previous_value`
- New field not in stored version: `status: "new"`
- Field in stored version but not in follow-up: your call. Document your reasoning.

The follow-up also has a top-level `missing_fields` array (fields the AI couldn't extract). Surface this in the merged response.

### Requirements

- Spring Boot, Java 17+
- Sensible package structure (controllers, services, models, your call on the split)
- Input validation with meaningful error responses (400, 404)
- At least 3 unit tests covering merge logic edge cases
- Runnable with `./gradlew bootRun` or `mvn spring-boot:run`
- README with 4 `curl` examples, one per endpoint

### Non-goals

No authentication. No database. No production logging setup. Don't over-engineer.

---

## Phase 1B: Make it operable (~60 min, before the session)

A backend that only runs on your laptop is half a backend. We need the infrastructure to run, exercise, monitor, and back up your service. Treat this as the artifacts you'd hand to an on-call engineer at 2am.

### Required deliverables

1. **`Dockerfile`** for the Spring Boot service. Multi-stage build, reasonable image size, non-root runtime user, pinned base image versions.

2. **`docker-compose.yml`** that brings up the service with any dependencies you've introduced. None are required, but if you've added Redis or a real DB, include it. Include a healthcheck definition.

3. **`ops/run.sh`** with subcommands: `build`, `start`, `stop`, `test`, `logs`, `clean`. Argument parsing with `--help` output. Proper exit codes. Should fail gracefully if Docker isn't running.

4. **`ops/backup.sh`** that fetches all known cases from the running service via `curl` and `jq`, writes them to a timestamped JSON file under `backups/`, logs to stderr with timestamps, and exits non-zero on failure. Should be safe to schedule as a cron job (no interactive prompts, no surprises).

5. **`ops/restore.sh`** that takes a backup file as argument, POSTs each case back to the service, and supports a `--dry-run` flag that shows what would happen without making any changes. Idempotent.

6. **`Makefile`** with `.PHONY` targets covering common operations. Fine if it just delegates to `ops/run.sh` for most things.

7. **README section titled "Operations"** as a runbook. Cover: how to build and deploy, how to verify the service is healthy, how to back up and restore data, how to debug a failed startup, what to check first if requests are failing. Write it as the doc you'd actually want at 2am.

### What we're looking for

- Real shell scripting craft, not Claude Code output you didn't read
- Sensible Docker patterns (small images, layer caching, non-root user, pinned versions)
- Defensive scripts (proper variable quoting, error handling on every external call, idempotency where it matters)
- Operational thinking (what could go wrong, how would I debug it, what would surprise me)

### Non-goals

No Kubernetes manifests. No Terraform. No real cloud deployment. Just clean, defensive local operability.

---

## Phase 2: Live React session (45 min build, 10 min walkthrough)

You'll build the reviewer's screen, calling your own Java backend from Phase 1.

### Setup before the call

- Your Java backend running locally on a known port (use your `ops/run.sh start`)
- A fresh React project (Next.js, Vite, or CRA, your choice). Empty is fine, just have the toolchain ready.
- Same GitHub repo as Phase 1, but a new folder (`/frontend`)
- `case_v2_followup_payload.json` (attached) ready to POST to your backend at the start of the session, so the merged case is queryable

### What to build

**Must-have**

1. Fetch the merged case from `GET /cases/{caseId}` on load.
2. Display extracted fields grouped by section: Patient, Suspect Drug, Adverse Event, Reporter.
3. For each field show: label, value, AI confidence score, source reference (e.g., "p.4 §1").
4. Color-code confidence: low (<0.80), medium (0.80 to 0.90), high (>0.90).
5. Conflict view: when a field has `status: "overridden"`, show both the new value and the previous value side-by-side, with the new value visually primary.
6. "Raise Query" button on conflicting fields. Opens a modal with a textarea and submit. Submit POSTs to your `/queries` endpoint.
7. Case Classification selector at the top: significant, non-significant, null. Editable in UI (no backend persistence needed for this).
8. Sort and filter: sort fields by AI confidence (low first), filter to show only conflicting fields.

**Bonus (only if time allows)**

9. Status pills on each field: New, Overridden, Unchanged.
10. Surface the `missing_fields` array prominently.
11. Empty, loading, and error states for the API calls.
12. Keyboard navigation between fields.

### Constraints

- Brand colors: navy `#0C1A36`, brand blue `#0077B6`, accent teal `#00C2E0`. Use sensibly, no need to overdesign.
- Commit every 10 to 15 minutes so we can see the progression.
- Backend down? Fallback to importing the JSON directly so the frontend work isn't blocked. Flag it as a known issue.

---

## Repo and logistics

- Create one public GitHub repo for everything. Folders: `/backend`, `/ops`, `/frontend`.
- Invite [@gargi-github-handle] as a collaborator.
- Push Phase 1A and 1B before the live session.
- Commit frequently in all phases.
- After the live session: save your Claude Code session log (located at `~/.claude/projects/<encoded-project-path>/<session-id>.jsonl` on macOS/Linux, `%USERPROFILE%\.claude\projects\...` on Windows) and commit the latest one to `/claude-code-session.jsonl` or share via email. We use it as part of the evaluation.

### Time budget

- Phase 1A (Java backend): ~90 min
- Phase 1B (Operability): ~60 min
- Phase 2 (Live React): 45 min + 10 min walkthrough

Total focused work: about 2.5 hours of take-home, plus 55 minutes live. You have 48 hours of clock time before the live session for Phase 1.

---

## What we're looking for

- **Process.** How you scope, scaffold, and iterate with Claude Code.
- **Product judgment.** What you ask before building, what assumptions you make explicit, what you add unprompted.
- **Backend craft.** Package structure, validation, error handling, test coverage, API design.
- **Operability craft.** Docker patterns, shell scripting discipline, defensive automation, runbook quality.
- **Frontend craft.** Component decomposition, state management, accessibility instincts, UX polish under time pressure.
- **Full-stack coherence.** Does your API design make the frontend pleasant or painful to build? Does your runbook match how the service actually behaves?
- **Communication.** Narrate while you build (live session). Write the runbook for a stranger (take-home).

---

## Files attached

- `case_v1.json`: bootstrap data for your Java backend
- `case_v2_followup_payload.json`: the follow-up you'll POST at the start of the live session

## Questions?

Email anything ahead of time. During the live session, ask freely.

Good luck. Have fun with it.

]

And here's the seed data file called case_v1.json that the backend will load on startup:

Read both. Don't build anything yet. Tell me:
1. What you understood about Phase 1A
2. What your thinking is on the "your call" merge case — field exists in stored version but not in the follow-up payload
3. How you'd structure the Spring Boot project — what packages, what files

I want to align on the approach before we touch any code.

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

content.text:
Read both. Here's my read before we write anything.

## 1. What I understood about Phase 1A

A Spring Boot service, no DB, that:
- Boots with `case_v1.json` loaded into memory as `PV-2026-0451` v1.
- Exposes `GET /cases/{id}`, `POST /cases/{id}/follow-ups`, `POST /queries`, `GET /queries?caseId=`, `GET /health`.
- The interesting logic is the merge: diff a follow-up payload against the stored case, per field, and annotate status.

Two things I had to infer because they're not fully spelled out, and I want to flag them as assumptions rather than silently bake them in:

**The follow-up payload is a full re-extraction, not a sparse delta.** The product context says "the AI re-extracts" (not "the AI sends updates"), and the merge rules include `unchanged` as a real outcome — that only makes sense if the follow-up re-includes fields that didn't change, so the engine can confirm them. If follow-ups were delta-only, `unchanged` would never fire. I'm building on the assumption that `case_v2_followup_payload.json` will look like a second `case_v1.json` (same `sections` shape), not a sparse patch. I don't have that file yet — only `case_v1.json` was in your message — so this is the one assumption I'd most want confirmed before Phase 2, since it's load-bearing for the whole merge engine.

**`GET /cases/{id}` has to return the *annotated* merged case, not a clean copy.** Phase 1A's spec reads like GET returns plain current values. But Phase 2 says: POST the follow-up at the start of the session, then "fetch the merged case from `GET /cases/{id}` on load" and render `status`/`previous_value` from it. So GET and the follow-up response can't be different shapes — whatever the follow-up endpoint computes has to become the persisted "current state," and GET just returns that. Concretely: the store holds one `Case` object per `caseId`; before any follow-up, every field's `status`/`previous_value` is just null; after a follow-up, the store is replaced by the freshly annotated version. A second follow-up diffs against that *last annotated state*, not against original v1 — diffs aren't cumulative across the whole history.

## 2. The "your call": field in stored version, absent from follow-up

I'd add a fourth status, `retained`, rather than folding this into `unchanged` or `new`.

Reasoning: `unchanged` should mean "the AI looked at this again and got the same answer" — it's a positive reconfirmation signal a reviewer can trust. If a field is silently absent from the follow-up, the AI didn't reconfirm anything; we just have no new information. Calling that `unchanged` overstates confidence in a regulated workflow where a reviewer is signing off on data provenance. So:

- Carry the stored value forward (never silently drop previously-validated data — that's the one hard rule I'd apply here).
- Mark it `retained` so the UI/reviewer can distinguish "AI confirmed this again" from "AI didn't touch this field this round."
- Cross-reference against the top-level `missing_fields` array but don't conflate the two: a field can be `retained` whether or not it's explicitly named in `missing_fields`. If it is named there, that's the AI explicitly saying "I tried and couldn't extract it" — useful extra context, surfaced as-is at the top level, not something I'd fold into the per-field status itself (keeps the status enum small and the missing_fields array doing its one job).

I'll document this explicitly in the README per the brief's ask, and write a dedicated unit test for it (including the edge case of a follow-up that omits an entire section, e.g. drops `reporter` wholesale — same per-field logic should still apply field-by-field).

## 3. Spring Boot structure

Repo-level (matches the brief's required top-level folders):
```
/backend          <- Spring Boot project root
/ops               run.sh, backup.sh, restore.sh
/frontend          (Phase 2)
docker-compose.yml, Makefile  <- repo root, orchestrate /backend
```

Inside `/backend`:
```
backend/
  build.gradle.kts, settings.gradle.kts        (Gradle — wrapper is the first option listed, faster incremental builds)
  Dockerfile
  src/main/java/com/pvcase/backend/
    BackendApplication.java
    controller/
      CaseController.java        GET /cases/{id}, POST /cases/{id}/follow-ups
      QueryController.java       POST /queries, GET /queries
      HealthController.java      GET /health  (plain custom controller, not Actuator — one endpoint, no need for the dependency, and I want exact control of the path/shape for the Docker healthcheck)
    service/
      CaseService.java           orchestration: lookup, store, version bump
      MergeService.java          the actual diff/merge algorithm — isolated so it's trivially unit-testable without Spring context
      QueryService.java
    model/
      Case.java                  record: caseId, version, classification, extractedAt, sourceDocument, sections, missingFields
      FieldRecord.java           record: value, confidence, source, status, previousValue (status/previousValue null until a merge touches it)
      MergeStatus.java           enum: UNCHANGED, OVERRIDDEN, NEW, RETAINED
      Query.java                 record: id, caseId, fieldPath, question, createdAt
    dto/
      FollowUpRequest.java       raw incoming shape (value/confidence/source only, no status) — mirrors case_v1.json shape, plus missingFields
      QueryRequest.java          caseId, fieldPath, question
      ErrorResponse.java         consistent 400/404 body shape
    repository/
      CaseStore.java             ConcurrentHashMap<String, Case> + a bootstrap loader
      QueryStore.java
    bootstrap/
      CaseDataLoader.java        ApplicationRunner, reads case_v1.json from resources at startup
    exception/
      CaseNotFoundException.java
      GlobalExceptionHandler.java  @ControllerAdvice → 400/404 mapping
  src/main/resources/
    application.yml
    data/case_v1.json
  src/test/java/.../service/
    MergeServiceTest.java        the bulk of the required 3+ tests live here
    CaseServiceTest.java
  README.md
```

A couple of small calls baked into that layout, open to pushback:
- **Java records, no Lombok.** Java 17 records cover all the DTO/model boilerplate here; didn't see a reason to pull in a dependency for it.
- **No separate "plain case" vs "merged case" model.** One `Case`/`FieldRecord` shape used everywhere (bootstrap, GET, follow-up response) — status/previousValue are just null pre-merge. Avoids two near-duplicate types and matches the GET-returns-annotated-state requirement from Phase 2.
- **`/health` is a hand-rolled controller, not Spring Boot Actuator** — one endpoint, exact path control, no extra dependency.

Let me know if you want to swap Gradle for Maven or push back on the `retained` status — otherwise I'll start scaffolding.

message.content:
Okay this looks great . I agree with the retained status reasoning, makes 
sense for a reviewer context.

One thing though — can we use Lombok instead of Java records? I'm more comfortable 
with @Data, @Builder, @Value etc. and it'll be cleaner for the model classes with 
builders. Add it to the dependencies.

For the package name, com.pvcase.backend feels a bit redundant. Pick something 
shorter, like com.pvreview or whatever you think fits.

Also some more questions -where are you putting case_v1.json? Directly under 
src/main/resources/ or in a subfolder like resources/data/? Just want to know 
so the loader path is clear.

Okay go ahead and scaffold the full project now. All files — even just empty stubs 
with the class names. Start with build.gradle, settings.gradle, application.yml, 
and the main Application class first. Then the rest of the structure.

content.text:
Quick answers to your questions, then I'll scaffold.

**Resources location:** `src/main/resources/data/case_v1.json` (subfolder, not directly under `resources/`) — keeps bootstrap data visually separate from `application.yml` and any future fixtures. The loader will reference it as `classpath:data/case_v1.json`, configurable via an `app.bootstrap.case-data-path` property in `application.yml` so the path isn't hardcoded in Java.

**Lombok + package rename:** done — `com.pvreview`, `@Data`/`@Builder` on all model/dto classes instead of records. One consequence: I'm renaming the dto I'd called `FollowUpRequest` to `CasePayload`, since the same raw shape (`value`/`confidence`/`source` per field, no status yet) is needed for *both* the bootstrap JSON and the follow-up request body — one dto, two call sites, instead of duplicating it.

Let me check the environment before generating the Gradle wrapper (want the real wrapper jar, not a hand-rolled one), then scaffold.

Boots clean. Now let's build the merge logic in MergeService.

This is the core part so let's be careful. Rules:
- Same value → status: UNCHANGED
- Different value → status: OVERRIDDEN, set previousValue to the old FieldRecord
- New field not in stored version → status: NEW
- Field in stored version but not in follow-up → status: RETAINED, keep the old value

The method should take the current Case (can be null for first load) and a CasePayload, 
and return a fully merged Case with all fields annotated.

Also missing_fields from CasePayload should just pass through to the merged Case.

Keep MergeService pure — no HTTP stuff, no repository calls. Just input in, 
merged Case out. I want to be able to unit test it without spinning up Spring.

Show me the full implementation.

content.text:
Compiles clean. Walk-through of what `merge(Case current, CasePayload payload)` does in `backend/src/main/java/com/pvreview/service/MergeService.java`:

**Per-field logic** (`mergeField`) — exactly your four rules, plus a guard:
- present in both, same value → `UNCHANGED`
- present in both, different value → `OVERRIDDEN`, `previousValue` = old value
- only in payload → `NEW`
- only in stored → `RETAINED`, value carried forward
- in neither (shouldn't happen, but a key could theoretically appear via a JSON-null edge case) → silently skipped rather than NPE'ing

**`current == null` (first load) falls out of the same code path, no special-casing needed.** An empty current means every stored-side lookup misses, so every field in the payload resolves to `NEW`. That's actually the right semantic for bootstrap: nothing was stored before, so everything is new. `caseId` and `version` resolve from the payload in that branch since there's no `current` to read them from.

**One thing I should flag rather than silently decide:** you said "set previousValue to the old FieldRecord" — I implemented it as just the old *value* (a `String`), not the full old `FieldRecord` (value+confidence+source). Reasoning: the original brief says "include previous_value" (singular), and the frontend spec only needs a value to show side-by-side, not a previous confidence/source. Our `FieldRecord.previousValue` field is already typed `String`, so this didn't require a model change. If you actually want the full old snapshot (e.g. for a richer audit trail), that's a real fork — it'd mean changing `previousValue` from `String` to a nested value/confidence/source object, and I'd want to do that deliberately rather than as a side effect. Let me know if you want that instead.

Other implementation notes worth knowing about:
- **Ordering is deterministic.** Section and field keys are unioned via `LinkedHashSet` — payload's order first, then any keys only present in `current` appended after. Matters for predictable test assertions and stable frontend rendering.
- **Version resolution:** uses `payload.version` if present, else `current.version + 1`, else `1`. Handles both "follow-up payload declares its own version" and "follow-up just sends fields, let the server bump it."
- **`missingFields` passes through exactly as you said** — no union with any prior missing list, just `payload.missingFields` (defaulted to empty list if null, so the frontend never has to null-check it).
- **Top-level metadata** (`caseClassification`, `extractedAt`, `sourceDocument`) — payload's value wins if present, otherwise falls back to `current`'s. Not in your spec explicitly, but felt like the obvious behavior to avoid wiping metadata on a sparse follow-up.

Next logical step is wiring this into `CaseService` and writing the unit tests in `MergeServiceTest` — didn't do either yet since you asked specifically for the `MergeService` implementation. Want me to continue to tests, or do you want to look this over first?

message.content:
Okay MergeService looks good. What do you think the next steps should be before 
we move to the controllers? Like what needs to be wired up first?

content.text:
Before controllers, three things need to exist so the controllers can stay thin (deserialize → call service → return):

**1. Storage layer** — `CaseStore` and `QueryStore`. Trivial: `ConcurrentHashMap` wrappers with `find`/`save`/`findAll`. Nothing to decide here, just needs to exist since `CaseService` depends on it.

**2. `CaseService` and `QueryService`** — the orchestration layer that owns "load from store, call `MergeService`, save back, throw `CaseNotFoundException` on misses." This is where one real decision lives:

> **Does bootstrap (`case_v1.json` → initial store state) go through `MergeService`, or bypass it?**
> `MergeService.merge(null, payload)` technically works for bootstrap — every field resolves to `NEW` since nothing was stored before. But I don't think that's the right label for it. `NEW`/`OVERRIDDEN`/`UNCHANGED`/`RETAINED` should mean "this is what changed in the last follow-up," not "this case didn't exist until now." If bootstrap runs through merge, a fresh `GET /cases/PV-2026-0451` — before any follow-up is ever posted — shows every single field tagged `NEW`, which will look like a bug to anyone reviewing the take-home. I'd rather have `CaseService` do a separate, simple raw→`Case` mapping for bootstrap (status `null` on every field, no diffing), and reserve `MergeService` strictly for actual follow-up events. Flagging this since it decides whether `CaseService` needs one method or two.

**3. JSON casing — needs to be nailed down before any endpoint touches real JSON.** `case_v1.json` is snake_case (`case_id`, `previous_value`, `missing_fields`), but our Java fields are camelCase. Without configuring this, deserializing the bootstrap file or a follow-up payload will silently fail to populate fields (Jackson won't match `case_id` to `caseId`), and our responses will come back camelCase, breaking the brief's documented field names. Fix is one line: `spring.jackson.property-naming-strategy: SNAKE_CASE` in `application.yml` — no per-field `@JsonProperty` annotations needed, and `Map<String,...>` keys (the section names like `suspect_drug`) pass through untouched regardless, so no extra work there.

One wrinkle this surfaces: the brief's own `POST /queries` example is written camelCase — `{caseId, fieldPath, question}` — inconsistent with the snake_case case data. I'd apply snake_case globally anyway and treat the queries example as loose prose shorthand rather than a literal contract, mainly so the whole API has one casing convention instead of switching per-endpoint. Worth a quick gut-check from you since it does deviate from the brief's literal text.

**Then:** wire `CaseDataLoader` to actually call `CaseService`'s bootstrap method (replacing the empty `run()`), and stand up `GlobalExceptionHandler` (404 for `CaseNotFoundException`, 400 for validation failures) so controllers have somewhere to throw to. Controllers come last and should be the easy part if this is right.

Want me to proceed in that order — storage, then `CaseService`/`QueryService` with the bootstrap-bypasses-merge split, then the Jackson config, then the loader and exception handler?

- `CaseService.bootstrap()` builds the baseline `Case` directly (null status everywhere), bypassing `MergeService` as agreed; `applyFollowUp()` is the only path that touches merge logic

- `GlobalExceptionHandler` is now `@RestControllerAdvice` with handlers for `CaseNotFoundException` (404), bean-validation failures (400), and malformed JSON bodies (400) — ready for controllers to throw into once `@Valid` annotations land on the DTOs

message.content:
Quick question before controllers — in the merge logic, what happens if the 
follow-up payload has a completely new section that didn't exist in the stored 
case? And what if the stored case has a section that's totally missing from the 
follow-up? Does the current mergeSections handle both those cases correctly?

lastPrompt: Quick question before controllers — in the merge logic, what happens if the  follow-up payload has a completely new section that didn't exist in the stored  case? And what if the stored case has a sec…

content.text:
Yes, both are handled correctly — and neither needed special-casing because section-level merging and field-level merging use the same union+fallback pattern recursively. Traced through the actual code:

**New section, not in stored case** (e.g. follow-up introduces `lab_results`, which `case_v1` never had):

In `mergeSections` (line 71), `orderedKeys` includes `lab_results` because it's in `payloadSections.keySet()`. At line 72, `currentFields = currentSections.getOrDefault("lab_results", emptyMap())` — since the stored case has no such section, this resolves to an **empty map**, not null. That empty map flows into `mergeFields`, where every field in the payload's `lab_results` section has `oldField == null` → falls into the `newField != null` branch at line 105 → status `NEW`. Same code path as a brand-new field inside an existing section, just triggered for every field at once.

**Stored section entirely missing from follow-up** (e.g. follow-up drops `reporter` wholesale):

`orderedKeys(payloadSections.keySet(), currentSections.keySet())` at line 71 still includes `reporter` — it's missing from the payload side but present in `currentSections.keySet()`, and `orderedKeys` unions both (payload's keys first, then any leftover current-only keys, line 122-126). At line 73, `payloadFields = payloadSections.getOrDefault("reporter", emptyMap())` resolves to an empty map. That flows into `mergeFields`, where every field in the stored `reporter` section has `newField == null` and `oldField != null` → falls through to the last branch (line 114) → status `RETAINED`, value carried forward from `oldField`.

So the `getOrDefault(sectionKey, Collections.emptyMap())` calls at the section level are what make this fall out for free — a missing section just looks like a section with zero fields on whichever side lacks it, and the existing per-field UNCHANGED/OVERRIDDEN/NEW/RETAINED logic does the rest without ever needing to know "this is a whole-section gap" versus "this is a single missing field." No gaps in the current implementation for either case.

This is exactly the scenario I'd planned for `MergeServiceTest` once we write tests — worth a dedicated case for "drops an entire section" since it's the kind of thing that's easy to silently break with a future refactor (e.g. someone "simplifying" the section loop to only iterate `payloadSections.keySet()` would quietly delete this behavior).

content.text: Wait — before concluding anything, I suspect my own test command, not the merge logic, broke this. I chained the curl with `|| <fallback curl>` to pretty-print via `python3 -m json.tool`, but if that pretty-printer fails for any reason, the `||` fallback re-runs curl — meaning the **same POST might have fired twice** against a non-idempotent endpoint. That would exactly explain `age` and `severity_grade` coming back `UNCHANGED`: the second application of the same payload would see no diff against the just-merged result. Let me verify by restarting clean and re-testing with a single request.

message.content:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

<summary>
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The user is building a take-home Spring Boot backend for a pharmacovigilance (PV) case review platform (`PV-2026-0451`), following a multi-phase brief: Phase 1A (Java/Spring Boot backend with merge logic for follow-up ICSR case data), Phase 1B (Docker/ops scripts — not yet started), and Phase 2 (React frontend — not yet started). Within Phase 1A, the explicit, sequential requests so far were:
   - Discuss approach only, no code, covering: understanding of Phase 1A, reasoning on the "field in stored but missing from follow-up" merge ambiguity, and proposed Spring Boot package structure.
   - After agreeing to the `RETAINED` status design, switch to Lombok (`@Data`/`@Builder`) instead of Java records, rename package to something shorter than `com.pvcase.backend` (settled on `com.pvreview`), confirm `case_v1.json` location, then scaffold the entire backend project structure as files/stubs (build files first, then everything else).
   - Implement the full `MergeService` merge logic per

If you need specific details from before compaction (like exact code snippets, error messages, or content you generated), read the full transcript at: /Users/ashitaairan/.claude/projects/-Users-ashitaairan-Documents-pv-case-review/dbea81d5-9c8d-465e-934a-a8857a479d61.jsonl
Continue the conversation from where it left off without asking the user any further questions. Resume directly — do not acknowledge the summary, do not recap what was happening, do not preface with "I'll continue" or similar. Pick up the last task as if the break never happened.

content.text:
Two things to sort out — not app bugs, but my test inputs:
1. `POST /queries` validation "failure" is expected: I sent camelCase JSON (`caseId`, `fieldPath`) matching the brief's literal example, but our global `SNAKE_CASE` Jackson strategy (which we agreed applies everywhere, including queries) expects `case_id`/`field_path` in the body. The validation behaved correctly given snake_case input — I just sent it wrong.
2. The `GET /queries?caseId=...` "error" was zsh glob-expanding the unquoted `?` in the URL, not the app.

Note `caseId` stays camelCase as a **query string parameter** (`?caseId=...`) — Jackson's naming strategy only governs JSON body (de)serialization, not `@RequestParam` binding, so the brief's literal `GET /queries?caseId={id}` is unaffected by our snake_case decision. Re-running correctly:

echo "--- POST /queries valid (snake_case body) ---"
curl -s -o /tmp/query_response.json -w "HTTP_STATUS:%{http_code}\n" -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"case_id":"PV-2026-0451","field_path":"patient.age","question":"Why did age change from 62 to 63?"}' \
  http://localhost:8080/queries
jq . /tmp/query_response.json
echo "--- POST /queries blank question (expect 400) ---"
curl -s -o /tmp/query_invalid.json -w "HTTP_STATUS:%{http_code}\n" -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"case_id":"PV-2026-0451","field_path":"patient.age","question":""}' \
  http://localhost:8080/queries
jq . /tmp/query_invalid.json
echo "--- GET /queries?caseId=... (quoted) ---"
curl -s "http://localhost:8080/queries?caseId=PV-2026-0451" | jq .

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

content.text:
All 17 tests pass (12 in `MergeServiceTest`, 5 in `CaseServiceTest`), covering `UNCHANGED`/`OVERRIDDEN`/`RETAINED`/`NEW` statuses, new vs. omitted sections, version handling, classification fallback/override, `missing_fields` passthrough, and `CaseService` orchestration including the not-found path.

**Summary of this stretch:** the apparent merge bug was a false alarm caused by my own test command (a `curl | json.tool || fallback-curl` double-POST against the non-idempotent follow-up endpoint); a clean single request confirmed the merge logic is correct. I then did a full endpoint sweep (health, get/post case, follow-ups 404/400, queries CRUD/validation/400) — all correct, modulo two more test-input mistakes on my end (camelCase vs. our agreed snake_case body convention, and an unquoted `?` in zsh). Finally, I replaced the empty `MergeServiceTest`/`CaseServiceTest` stubs with real JUnit coverage; full suite is green.

Phase 1A's core backend (model, merge logic, controllers, validation, error handling, tests) is in solid shape. Next up would be Phase 1B (Docker/ops scripts) or Phase 2 (React frontend) — let me know which to start.

message.content: Can u explain me the tests that you have written

content.text:
## `MergeServiceTest` (12 tests)

**Fixture (`setUp`)**: builds a `baseline` `Case` mimicking the stored v1 record — `patient` (initials, age, sex, weight_kg), `adverse_event` (event_term, onset_date, outcome, seriousness), `suspect_drug` (drug_name) — at version 1, classification `"non-significant"`. Every test starts from this same baseline and feeds it a minimal `CasePayload` (the "follow-up") into `mergeService.merge(baseline, payload)`, then asserts on the resulting `Case`.

Each test isolates one merge rule:

| Test | What it proves |
|---|---|
| `sameValueIsMarkedUnchanged` | Follow-up repeats `sex: "Male"` → status `UNCHANGED`, `previousValue` stays `null`. |
| `differingValueIsOverriddenAndKeepsPreviousValue` | Follow-up sends `age: "63"` vs stored `"62"` → status `OVERRIDDEN`, and critically `previousValue` is set to `"62"` so the audit trail isn't lost. |
| `fieldAbsentFromFollowUpIsRetainedFromStoredCase` | Follow-up payload doesn't mention `weight_kg` at all → it's carried forward unchanged with status `RETAINED` (this is the "field in stored but missing from follow-up" ambiguity we discussed designing for). |
| `newFieldInExistingSectionIsMarkedNew` | Follow-up adds `severity_grade` to the existing `adverse_event` section → `NEW`, while the untouched sibling field (`event_term`) in that same section still comes back `RETAINED`. |
| `entirelyNewSectionIsMarkedNew` | Follow-up introduces a whole new section (`lab_results.ck_level`) that didn't exist in the baseline at all → `NEW`. |
| `sectionOmittedFromFollowUpIsRetainedFieldByField` | Follow-up never mentions `suspect_drug` at all → every field in that section is preserved with `RETAINED`, proving the merge walks missing *sections* the same way it walks missing *fields*. |
| `versionIncrementsWhenNotExplicitlyProvided` | No `version` in the payload → auto-incremented from 1 to 2. |
| `explicitVersionInPayloadOverridesAutoIncrement` | Payload explicitly sets `version: 5` → that wins over auto-increment. |
| `caseClassificationFallsBackToStoredValueWhenOmitted` | Payload omits `case_classification` → keeps the stored `"non-significant"`. |
| `caseClassificationIsOverriddenWhenProvided` | Payload sets `case_classification: "significant"` → it overrides the stored value. |
| `missingFieldsFromPayloadArePassedThrough` | Payload's `missing_fields` list flows straight into the merged `Case`. |
| `missingFieldsDefaultToEmptyWhenOmitted` | If the payload omits `missing_fields`, it defaults to an empty list rather than `null`. |

Together these 12 cover all four `MergeStatus` values, both granularities of "missing" (field-level and section-level), and the three header fields that have fallback-vs-override behavior (`version`, `caseClassification`, `missingFields`).

## `CaseServiceTest` (5 tests)

This exercises the orchestration layer with a **mocked** `CaseStore` (via Mockito) but a **real** `MergeService`, since the merge logic itself is already covered above — here we only care that `CaseService` wires things together correctly.

| Test | What it proves |
|---|---|
| `getCaseReturnsStoredCase` | `getCase` just delegates to `caseStore.find` and returns what it finds. |
| `getCaseThrowsWhenNotFound` | Unknown `caseId` → `CaseNotFoundException` with the id in the message (this is what the `GlobalExceptionHandler` turns into the 404 we saw in smoke testing). |
| `applyFollowUpThrowsWhenCaseNotFound` | Same not-found behavior on the follow-up path, **and** verifies `caseStore.save(...)` is never called — i.e., a failed lookup must short-circuit before any write. |
| `applyFollowUpMergesAndSavesResult` | The "happy path": confirms `applyFollowUp` fetches current state, runs it through `mergeService.merge`, and persists the *exact* merged object via `caseStore.save`. |
| `bootstrapBuildsBaselineCaseWithNoStatusAndSavesIt` | Bootstrapping from `case_v1.json` builds `FieldRecord`s with `value`/`confidence`/`source` copied over but `status` left `null` (since a baseline field was never "merged" against anything — that's intentional, not a bug). |

The not-found tests are really regression guards for the 404 behavior we hand-verified with curl earlier; the merge-status tests are regression guards for the exact `OVERRIDDEN`/`NEW`/`RETAINED` behavior that the earlier double-POST mishap initially made look broken.

content: You're building the Phase 1A Spring Boot backend for the PV case review platform; merge logic and endpoints are verified, and I just added real JUnit tests (17 passing) replacing the empty stubs. Next: decide whether to move to Phase 1B (Docker/ops) or Phase 2 (React frontend). (disable recaps in /config)

lastPrompt: One small thing — the validation error message for a missing question field  comes back as "question question is required" — the field name is being  prepended twice. Can you fix that?

content.text: Found it — `GlobalExceptionHandler.handleValidation` builds the message as `error.getField() + " " + error.getDefaultMessage()`, but the `@NotBlank` messages in `QueryRequest` already include the field name, so it gets prepended twice. Fixing by removing the redundant field name from the annotation messages, letting the handler supply it once.

content.text: Fixed and verified — `"question is required"` now, no duplication, and the multi-field case reads cleanly too: `"caseId is required; fieldPath is required; question is required"`. Stopping the server since we're done testing.

input.command: pkill -f "gradlew bootRun" 2>/dev/null; pkill -f "PvReviewApplication" 2>/dev/null; sleep 1; lsof -i :8080 2>/dev/null || echo "port 8080 free"

content.text: The fix was one line in `QueryRequest.java`: each `@NotBlank` message changed from `"<field> is required"` to just `"is required"`, since `GlobalExceptionHandler` already prepends `error.getField()`. Tests still pass (17/17), and live curl checks confirm the message is now correct for both single- and multi-field validation failures.

# PV Case Review — Backend

input.content:
# PV Case Review — Backend

Spring Boot service for reviewing pharmacovigilance (PV) cases and merging in
follow-up reports. No database — cases and reviewer queries live in memory
for the lifetime of the process.

## Stack

- Java 17, Spring Boot 3.3, Gradle (wrapper included)
- Lombok for model/DTO boilerplate
- JSON request/response bodies use `snake_case` everywhere (`case_id`,
  `field_path`, etc.) via a global Jackson naming strategy — fields that are
  `null` are omitted from responses rather than serialized as `null`

## Running it

```bash
./gradlew bootRun
```

The app starts on `http://localhost:8080`. On startup it bootstraps one case,
**`PV-2026-0451`**, from `src/main/resources/data/case_v1.json`, so there's
something to query immediately — no seeding step required.

## Running the tests

```bash
./gradlew test
```

## The four main endpoints

### 1. Get a case

```bash
curl http://localhost:8080/cases/PV-2026-0451
```

```json
{
  "case_id": "PV-2026-0451",
  "version": 1,
  "case_classification": "non-significant",
  "extracted_at": "2026-04-08T09:14:00Z",
  "source_document": "initial_report_PV-2026-0451.pdf",
  "sections": {
    "patient": {
      "age": { "value": "62", "confidence": 0.91, "source": "p.2 §1" },
      "weight_kg": { "value": "78", "confidence": 0.85, "source": "p.3 §2" }
    }
  },
  "missing_fields": []
}
```

Unknown case id → `404` with an `ErrorResponse` body.

### 2. Apply a follow-up report (merge)

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/cases/PV-2026-0451/follow-ups \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "case_classification": "significant",
    "sections": {
      "patient": {
        "age": { "value": "63", "confidence": 0.90, "source": "p.2 §1" }
      },
      "adverse_event": {
        "severity_grade": { "value": "Grade 2", "confidence": 0.83, "source": "p.4 §3" }
      },
      "lab_results": {
        "ck_level": { "value": "450 U/L", "confidence": 0.89, "source": "p.6 §1" }
      }
    },
    "missing_fields": ["patient.weight_kg"]
  }'
```

```json
{
  "case_id": "PV-2026-0451",
  "version": 2,
  "case_classification": "significant",
  "sections": {
    "patient": {
      "age": { "value": "63", "confidence": 0.90, "source": "p.2 §1", "status": "OVERRIDDEN", "previous_value": "62" },
      "weight_kg": { "value": "78", "confidence": 0.85, "source": "p.3 §2", "status": "RETAINED" }
    },
    "adverse_event": {
      "severity_grade": { "value": "Grade 2", "confidence": 0.83, "source": "p.4 §3", "status": "NEW" }
    },
    "lab_results": {
      "ck_level": { "value": "450 U/L", "confidence": 0.89, "source": "p.6 §1", "status": "NEW" }
    }
  },
  "missing_fields": ["patient.weight_kg"]
}
```

`version` auto-increments if you don't supply one. Unknown case id → `404`;
malformed JSON body → `400`.

### 3. Submit a reviewer query against a field

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/queries \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "case_id": "PV-2026-0451",
    "field_path": "patient.age",
    "question": "Why did age change from 62 to 63?"
  }'
```

```json
{
  "id": "6f9ee839-db97-49e4-90e6-260761a210c1",
  "case_id": "PV-2026-0451",
  "field_path": "patient.age",
  "question": "Why did age change from 62 to 63?",
  "created_at": "2026-06-21T10:04:53.312608Z"
}
```

Any blank field → `400` (e.g. `"question is required"`).

### 4. List queries for a case

```bash
curl "http://localhost:8080/queries?caseId=PV-2026-0451"
```

```json
[
  {
    "id": "6f9ee839-db97-49e4-90e6-260761a210c1",
    "case_id": "PV-2026-0451",
    "field_path": "patient.age",
    "question": "Why did age change from 62 to 63?",
    "created_at": "2026-06-21T10:04:53.312608Z"
  }
]
```

Note: `caseId` here is a URL query parameter, not a JSON body field, so it
stays camelCase — the `snake_case` rule only applies to JSON bodies.

*(There's also a `GET /health` liveness check, returning `{"status": "UP"}`.)*

## Merge behavior

Every field on a case carries a `status` describing how it got its current
value, computed each time a follow-up is applied:

| Status | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `NEW` | Field (or whole section) didn't exist before; the follow-up introduced it. |
| `OVERRIDDEN` | Field existed before with a different value; the follow-up replaced it. `previous_value` holds what it used to be. |
| `UNCHANGED` | Field existed before with the *same* value; the follow-up just re-confirmed it. |
| `RETAINED` | Field existed before and the follow-up **didn't mention it at all**; the stored value carries forward as-is. |

### Why `RETAINED` exists

A follow-up report only contains the fields the extractor found evidence for
in *that* document — it's a partial payload, not a full restatement of the
case. If a field is missing from the follow-up, that's almost always because
the new document simply didn't re-cover it, not because it should be erased.
Treating "absent from follow-up" the same as "explicitly removed" would
silently destroy data from earlier reports, which is the wrong default for
PV case data where every value is sourced and audited.

So instead of overwriting or dropping anything, the merge keeps the stored
field's `value`/`confidence`/`source` untouched and tags it `RETAINED`,
making it visible to a reviewer as "carried forward, not reconfirmed" rather
than indistinguishable from a freshly-extracted field. This applies at both
granularities: a single field can be retained inside an otherwise-updated
section (e.g. `patient.weight_kg` above), and a whole section omitted from
the follow-up is retained field-by-field, not dropped wholesale.

## Error responses

All errors share one shape:

```json
{
  "status": 404,
  "error": "Not Found",
  "message": "No case found with id UNKNOWN-ID",
  "path": "/cases/UNKNOWN-ID",
  "timestamp": "2026-06-21T10:07:46.857813Z"
}
```

| Situation | Status |
|---|---|
| Unknown `caseId` | `404` |
| Malformed JSON body | `400` |
| Missing/blank required field (e.g. in `/queries`) | `400` |
| Missing required query param (e.g. `caseId` on `GET /queries`) | `400` |

+  `field_path`, etc.) via a global Jackson naming strategy — fields that are
+  `null` are omitted from responses rather than serialized as `null`
+The app starts on `http://localhost:8080`. On startup it bootstraps one case,
+something to query immediately — no seeding step required.
+`version` auto-increments if you don't supply one. Unknown case id → `404`;
+### 3. Submit a reviewer query against a field
+Note: `caseId` here is a URL query parameter, not a JSON body field, so it
+stays camelCase — the `snake_case` rule only applies to JSON bodies.
+Every field on a case carries a `status` describing how it got its current
+value, computed each time a follow-up is applied:
+| `NEW` | Field (or whole section) didn't exist before; the follow-up introduced it. |
+| `OVERRIDDEN` | Field existed before with a different value; the follow-up replaced it. `previous_value` holds what it used to be. |
+| `UNCHANGED` | Field existed before with the *same* value; the follow-up just re-confirmed it. |
+| `RETAINED` | Field existed before and the follow-up **didn't mention it at all**; the stored value carries forward as-is. |
+A follow-up report only contains the fields the extractor found evidence for
+in *that* document — it's a partial payload, not a full restatement of the
+case. If a field is missing from the follow-up, that's almost always because
+the new document simply didn't re-cover it, not because it should be erased.
+Treating "absent from follow-up" the same as "explicitly removed" would
+silently destroy data from earlier reports, which is the wrong default for
+PV case data where every value is sourced and audited.
+So instead of overwriting or dropping anything, the merge keeps the stored
+making it visible to a reviewer as "carried forward, not reconfirmed" rather
+than indistinguishable from a freshly-extracted field. This applies at both
+granularities: a single field can be retained inside an otherwise-updated
+section (e.g. `patient.weight_kg` above), and a whole section omitted from
+the follow-up is retained field-by-field, not dropped wholesale.

message.content:
I'm thinking about how backup.sh and restore.sh 
will work and I realized there's a problem.

backup.sh needs to save all cases but right now there's no way to list all 
cases — only GET /cases/{id} which needs a known ID upfront. And restore.sh 
needs to PUT cases back exactly as they were, but if I use the follow-up 
endpoint it'll run merge logic and mess things up.

How would you handle this?

content.text:
Two separate gaps, two separate fixes:

1. **Listing cases** — add `GET /cases` that returns all cases in full (not just IDs). Since `CaseStore` is already just a `Map<String, Case>`, this is a one-line `findAll()` plus a controller method. `backup.sh` then just calls it once and dumps the array — no per-ID loop needed.

2. **Restoring without merge** — add `PUT /cases/{caseId}` as a *separate write path* that overwrites the stored `Case` verbatim (upsert: create if absent, replace if present), bypassing `MergeService` entirely. This matters because `status`/`previous_value` are actually persisted as part of the `Case` (computed once during merge, then saved), not derived live on read — so restore must write back the exact snapshot including those statuses, not re-derive them by replaying it through follow-up logic.

The tradeoff: you end up with two different ways to write a case — `POST /follow-ups` (business operation, merge semantics apply) vs. `PUT /cases/{caseId}` (ops operation, raw overwrite, no merge) — so it's worth being explicit in the code/README that the `PUT` path is for backup/restore only and isn't a "way to update a case" for normal use.

Want me to go ahead and add these two endpoints?

content.text:
Both endpoints are in and fully verified end-to-end:

- **`GET /cases`** — `CaseController.getAllCases()` → `CaseService.getAllCases()` → `CaseStore.findAll()`. `backup.sh` can hit this once and dump the array; each case includes its full `sections`/`status`/`previous_value` state, so nothing is lost.
- **`PUT /cases/{caseId}`** — writes a `Case` snapshot back verbatim, completely bypassing `MergeService`. Marked with an explicit comment at both the controller and service method: `// Ops restore only (backup.sh/restore.sh) — not a normal update path; use POST /{caseId}/follow-ups for that.` It upserts (creates if the ID doesn't exist, overwrites if it does), and guards against a path/body `case_id` mismatch with a `400`.

Verified live: backed up a case mid-merge (`version 2`, `age` `OVERRIDDEN` with `previous_value: "62"`), mutated it further (`version 3`), restored the `version 2` backup via `PUT`, and `diff`'d the result against the original backup — byte-for-byte identical, confirming restore never touches the merge logic. Mismatch rejection and upsert-into-new-ID also confirmed. Full test suite (21 tests now) passes.

This gives `backup.sh`/`restore.sh` exactly the two primitives they need — enumerate, and write-back-exactly — without ever routing through the follow-up/merge endpoint.

content: Building the PV case review backend's ops support: just added GET /cases and PUT /cases/{caseId} for backup/restore, both tested and verified working. Next: write backup.sh and restore.sh scripts that use them.

content.text:
For Java 17, I'd pick **Eclipse Temurin** for both stages — it's the official Adoptium OpenJDK build, well-maintained, and it's actually already what's on this machine (`/Library/Java/JavaVirtualMachines/temurin-17.jdk`), so dev and container builds match exactly:

- **Build stage:** `eclipse-temurin:17-jdk-jammy` — full JDK on Ubuntu Jammy (glibc). This stage gets discarded after the build, so its size doesn't matter; glibc avoids any musl-related quirks with Gradle's toolchain.
- **Runtime stage:** `eclipse-temurin:17-jre-alpine` — JRE-only, smallest option. Since this app has zero native/JNI dependencies (pure Spring Boot REST + in-memory store), Alpine's musl libc isn't a risk here, and it directly serves your "keep the image small" requirement.

Both tags pin the OS variant explicitly (`jammy` / `alpine`) rather than floating on Temurin's default base — that satisfies "pinned, not `:latest`" without me having to guess an exact patch+build number I can't verify still exists.

Writing the Dockerfile now.

input.content:
# ---- Build stage ----
FROM eclipse-temurin:17-jdk-jammy AS build
WORKDIR /app

COPY gradlew settings.gradle build.gradle ./
COPY gradle ./gradle
RUN ./gradlew dependencies --no-daemon

COPY src ./src
RUN ./gradlew bootJar --no-daemon

# ---- Runtime stage ----
FROM eclipse-temurin:17-jre-alpine AS runtime
WORKDIR /app

RUN addgroup -S pvreview && adduser -S pvreview -G pvreview
COPY --from=build /app/build/libs/*.jar app.jar
RUN chown pvreview:pvreview app.jar
USER pvreview

EXPOSE 8080
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=3s --start-period=20s --retries=3 \
  CMD wget --no-verbose --tries=1 --spider http://localhost:8080/health || exit 1

ENTRYPOINT ["java", "-jar", "app.jar"]

content.text: Found a real platform issue: `eclipse-temurin:17-jre-alpine` only ships an `amd64` manifest — no `arm64` build, so it can't even be pulled on this Apple Silicon Mac. The jammy variants (`jdk-jammy`, `jre-jammy`) do support `arm64`. Switching the runtime stage to `eclipse-temurin:17-jre-jammy` — still satisfies "JRE not JDK" and is meaningfully smaller than the JDK image, just not as small as Alpine would've been.

file.content:
# ---- Build stage ----
FROM eclipse-temurin:17-jdk-jammy AS build
WORKDIR /app

COPY gradlew settings.gradle build.gradle ./
COPY gradle ./gradle
RUN ./gradlew dependencies --no-daemon

COPY src ./src
RUN ./gradlew bootJar --no-daemon

# ---- Runtime stage ----
FROM eclipse-temurin:17-jre-jammy AS runtime
WORKDIR /app

RUN groupadd --system pvreview && useradd --system --gid pvreview pvreview
COPY --from=build /app/build/libs/*.jar app.jar
RUN chown pvreview:pvreview app.jar
USER pvreview

EXPOSE 8080
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=3s --start-period=20s --retries=3 \
  CMD wget --no-verbose --tries=1 --spider http://localhost:8080/health || exit 1

ENTRYPOINT ["java", "-jar", "app.jar"]

lastPrompt: Okay starting Phase 1B now. Let's do the Dockerfile first.  I need a multi-stage build — one stage for building the jar, one stage for  running it. Runtime should use JRE not JDK to keep the image sma…

content.text:
**One thing worth flagging:** I initially picked `eclipse-temurin:17-jre-alpine` for the runtime stage as discussed, but it failed to even pull — that tag has no `arm64` manifest, only `amd64`, so it can't build on this Apple Silicon Mac. Switched to `eclipse-temurin:17-jre-jammy` instead, which supports both architectures. Trade-off: less small than Alpine would've been, but it actually builds and runs here, which Alpine couldn't.

Verified end-to-end:
- `docker build` succeeds (multi-stage: `gradlew dependencies` cached separately from `src` for layer caching, then `bootJar` — deliberately not `build`/`assemble`, which would also run the test suite and risk producing a stray `-plain.jar` alongside the boot jar)
- Image size: **446 MB**
- Runs as **non-root**: `uid=999(pvreview) gid=999(pvreview)`
- `GET /health` → `{"status":"UP"}`, and Docker's own `HEALTHCHECK` reports `healthy`
- The bootstrapped case `PV-2026-0451` is reachable through the container

Also added a `.dockerignore` (`build/`, `.gradle/`, etc.) so the build context doesn't drag in ~800KB of local Gradle caches/artifacts.

Next up for Phase 1B: `backup.sh` and `restore.sh` themselves, using the `GET /cases` and `PUT /cases/{caseId}` endpoints we built earlier.

message.content:
Wait, before backup.sh — let's do docker-compose.yml first. The brief asks 
for it separately. Just the backend service, include a healthcheck. What 
should restart policy be for a local dev setup?

content.text:
For local dev specifically, I'd set `restart: "no"` (explicit, not omitted) — not `unless-stopped` or `always`. The reason is specific to this app: `CaseStore` is in-memory only, no persistence layer. If the container silently auto-restarts after a crash, you lose all accumulated state (any follow-ups/restores you'd applied) with no visible signal that it happened — which is exactly the kind of surprise you don't want while you're actively testing things like backup/restore behavior in the next phase. `unless-stopped`/`always` make sense for a service you depend on staying up unattended; that's not this.

Writing `docker-compose.yml` now with that restart policy.

services:

      retries: 3

`backend/ops/run.sh` is done and fully exercised:

| Subcommand | Behavior verified |

| `start` | `docker compose up -d` — container started, healthcheck went `healthy`, API reachable |
| `logs` | `docker compose logs -f` — streamed real container output |
| `stop` | `docker compose down` — container + network fully removed |
| `test` | `./gradlew test` — runs independently of Docker (confirmed working even with Docker faked as down) |

It resolves its own path via `BASH_SOURCE`, so it works no matter what directory you invoke it from. Next up: `backup.sh` and `restore.sh`.

message.content:
Now backup.sh — the brief says it should fetch all cases 
from the running service, write to a timestamped file under backups/, log 
to stderr with timestamps, and be safe to cron. What do you need from me 
before you write it?

lastPrompt: Now backup.sh — the brief says it should fetch all cases  from the running service, write to a timestamped file under backups/, log  to stderr with timestamps, and be safe to cron. What do you need fr…

Now backup.sh — the brief says it should fetch all cases  from the running service, write to a timestamped file under backups/, log  to stderr with timestamps, and be safe to cron. What do you need fr…

questions.question: Should backup.sh write one combined file per run, or split into one file per case?

options.label: Single combined file (Recommended)
options.description: backups/cases-<timestamp>.json holds the full array exactly as GET /cases returns it. Simplest, one atomic write, and restore.sh can just iterate the array to PUT each case back.

options.label: One file per case
options.description: backups/<timestamp>/<caseId>.json per case. More granular — lets you restore a single case without touching the rest — but adds directory creation and looping complexity to both scripts.

questions.question: Where will backup.sh actually run, so I know what URL it should hit?

options.label: Host machine, via published port (Recommended)
options.description: Hits http://localhost:8080 — matches the docker-compose.yml port mapping. Standard setup for a host crontab entry.

options.label: Inside the Docker network, as another container
options.description: Hits http://backend:8080 using the compose service name. Only relevant if backup.sh itself runs as a container on the same docker-compose network rather than on the host.

content.content: Your questions have been answered: "Should backup.sh write one combined file per run, or split into one file per case?"="Single combined file (Recommended)", "Where will backup.sh actually run, so I know what URL it should hit?"="Host machine, via published port (Recommended)". You can now continue with these answers in mind.

questions.question: Should backup.sh write one combined file per run, or split into one file per case?

options.label: Single combined file (Recommended)
options.description: backups/cases-<timestamp>.json holds the full array exactly as GET /cases returns it. Simplest, one atomic write, and restore.sh can just iterate the array to PUT each case back.

options.label: One file per case
options.description: backups/<timestamp>/<caseId>.json per case. More granular — lets you restore a single case without touching the rest — but adds directory creation and looping complexity to both scripts.

questions.question: Where will backup.sh actually run, so I know what URL it should hit?

options.label: Host machine, via published port (Recommended)
options.description: Hits http://localhost:8080 — matches the docker-compose.yml port mapping. Standard setup for a host crontab entry.

options.label: Inside the Docker network, as another container
options.description: Hits http://backend:8080 using the compose service name. Only relevant if backup.sh itself runs as a container on the same docker-compose network rather than on the host.

lastPrompt: Now backup.sh — the brief says it should fetch all cases  from the running service, write to a timestamped file under backups/, log  to stderr with timestamps, and be safe to cron. What do you need fr…
