toolUseResult.originalFile:
# PV Case Review — Backend

Spring Boot service for reviewing pharmacovigilance (PV) cases and merging in
follow-up reports. No database — cases and reviewer queries live in memory
for the lifetime of the process.

## Stack

- Java 17, Spring Boot 3.3, Gradle (wrapper included)
- Lombok for model/DTO boilerplate
- JSON request/response bodies use `snake_case` everywhere (`case_id`,
  `field_path`, etc.) via a global Jackson naming strategy — fields that are
  `null` are omitted from responses rather than serialized as `null`

## Running it

```bash
./gradlew bootRun
```

The app starts on `http://localhost:8080`. On startup it bootstraps one case,
**`PV-2026-0451`**, from `src/main/resources/data/case_v1.json`, so there's
something to query immediately — no seeding step required.

## Running the tests

```bash
./gradlew test
```

## The four main endpoints

### 1. Get a case

```bash
curl http://localhost:8080/cases/PV-2026-0451
```

```json
{
  "case_id": "PV-2026-0451",
  "version": 1,
  "case_classification": "non-significant",
  "extracted_at": "2026-04-08T09:14:00Z",
  "source_document": "initial_report_PV-2026-0451.pdf",
  "sections": {
    "patient": {
      "age": { "value": "62", "confidence": 0.91, "source": "p.2 §1" },
      "weight_kg": { "value": "78", "confidence": 0.85, "source": "p.3 §2" }
    }
  },
  "missing_fields": []
}
```

Unknown case id → `404` with an `ErrorResponse` body.

### 2. Apply a follow-up report (merge)

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/cases/PV-2026-0451/follow-ups \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "case_classification": "significant",
    "sections": {
      "patient": {
        "age": { "value": "63", "confidence": 0.90, "source": "p.2 §1" }
      },
      "adverse_event": {
        "severity_grade": { "value": "Grade 2", "confidence": 0.83, "source": "p.4 §3" }
      },
      "lab_results": {
        "ck_level": { "value": "450 U/L", "confidence": 0.89, "source": "p.6 §1" }
      }
    },
    "missing_fields": ["patient.weight_kg"]
  }'
```

```json
{
  "case_id": "PV-2026-0451",
  "version": 2,
  "case_classification": "significant",
  "sections": {
    "patient": {
      "age": { "value": "63", "confidence": 0.90, "source": "p.2 §1", "status": "OVERRIDDEN", "previous_value": "62" },
      "weight_kg": { "value": "78", "confidence": 0.85, "source": "p.3 §2", "status": "RETAINED" }
    },
    "adverse_event": {
      "severity_grade": { "value": "Grade 2", "confidence": 0.83, "source": "p.4 §3", "status": "NEW" }
    },
    "lab_results": {
      "ck_level": { "value": "450 U/L", "confidence": 0.89, "source": "p.6 §1", "status": "NEW" }
    }
  },
  "missing_fields": ["patient.weight_kg"]
}
```

`version` auto-increments if you don't supply one. Unknown case id → `404`;
malformed JSON body → `400`.

### 3. Submit a reviewer query against a field

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/queries \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "case_id": "PV-2026-0451",
    "field_path": "patient.age",
    "question": "Why did age change from 62 to 63?"
  }'
```

```json
{
  "id": "6f9ee839-db97-49e4-90e6-260761a210c1",
  "case_id": "PV-2026-0451",
  "field_path": "patient.age",
  "question": "Why did age change from 62 to 63?",
  "created_at": "2026-06-21T10:04:53.312608Z"
}
```

Any blank field → `400` (e.g. `"question is required"`).

### 4. List queries for a case

```bash
curl "http://localhost:8080/queries?caseId=PV-2026-0451"
```

```json
[
  {
    "id": "6f9ee839-db97-49e4-90e6-260761a210c1",
    "case_id": "PV-2026-0451",
    "field_path": "patient.age",
    "question": "Why did age change from 62 to 63?",
    "created_at": "2026-06-21T10:04:53.312608Z"
  }
]
```

Note: `caseId` here is a URL query parameter, not a JSON body field, so it
stays camelCase — the `snake_case` rule only applies to JSON bodies.

*(There's also a `GET /health` liveness check, returning `{"status": "UP"}`.)*

## Merge behavior

Every field on a case carries a `status` describing how it got its current
value, computed each time a follow-up is applied:

| Status | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `NEW` | Field (or whole section) didn't exist before; the follow-up introduced it. |
| `OVERRIDDEN` | Field existed before with a different value; the follow-up replaced it. `previous_value` holds what it used to be. |
| `UNCHANGED` | Field existed before with the *same* value; the follow-up just re-confirmed it. |
| `RETAINED` | Field existed before and the follow-up **didn't mention it at all**; the stored value carries forward as-is. |

### Why `RETAINED` exists

A follow-up report only contains the fields the extractor found evidence for
in *that* document — it's a partial payload, not a full restatement of the
case. If a field is missing from the follow-up, that's almost always because
the new document simply didn't re-cover it, not because it should be erased.
Treating "absent from follow-up" the same as "explicitly removed" would
silently destroy data from earlier reports, which is the wrong default for
PV case data where every value is sourced and audited.

So instead of overwriting or dropping anything, the merge keeps the stored
field's `value`/`confidence`/`source` untouched and tags it `RETAINED`,
making it visible to a reviewer as "carried forward, not reconfirmed" rather
than indistinguishable from a freshly-extracted field. This applies at both
granularities: a single field can be retained inside an otherwise-updated
section (e.g. `patient.weight_kg` above), and a whole section omitted from
the follow-up is retained field-by-field, not dropped wholesale.

## Error responses

All errors share one shape:

```json
{
  "status": 404,
  "error": "Not Found",
  "message": "No case found with id UNKNOWN-ID",
  "path": "/cases/UNKNOWN-ID",
  "timestamp": "2026-06-21T10:07:46.857813Z"
}
```

| Situation | Status |
|---|---|
| Unknown `caseId` | `404` |
| Malformed JSON body | `400` |
| Missing/blank required field (e.g. in `/queries`) | `400` |
| Missing required query param (e.g. `caseId` on `GET /queries`) | `400` |

## Operations

Running via Docker, plus backup/restore tooling under `ops/`. None of this
needs the JVM or Gradle on the host — only Docker.

### Running via Docker

```bash
docker compose up -d --build
```

Builds the image (multi-stage: JDK to compile, JRE to run, non-root user)
and starts the backend on `http://localhost:8080` — same app as
`./gradlew bootRun`, just containerized. `docker-compose.yml` sets
`restart: "no"` on purpose: `CaseStore` is in-memory, so an auto-restarting
container would come back up with an empty store, silently losing whatever
was there before the crash.

### `ops/run.sh` and the Makefile

`ops/run.sh` wraps Docker Compose and Gradle with the usual lifecycle
subcommands; the `Makefile` just calls it so you can use whichever you
prefer:

```bash
./ops/run.sh build   /  make build    # docker compose build
./ops/run.sh start   /  make start    # docker compose up -d
./ops/run.sh stop    /  make stop     # docker compose down
./ops/run.sh test    /  make test     # ./gradlew test (doesn't need Docker)
./ops/run.sh logs    /  make logs     # follow container logs
./ops/run.sh clean   /  make clean    # stop containers, remove the image, ./gradlew clean
./ops/run.sh --help  /  make help
```

Every subcommand except `test` and `--help` checks `docker info` first and
fails with a one-line error if Docker isn't running, instead of letting a
raw Docker error leak through.

### Backups and restores

`backup.sh` fetches every case from a running backend and writes them to a
single timestamped, gitignored file:

```bash
make backup
# [2026-06-21T11:51:11Z] Starting backup from http://localhost:8080/cases
# [2026-06-21T11:51:11Z] Backup complete: 1 case(s) written to backups/cases-20260621T115111Z-LfgkHWYu.json
```

`restore.sh` takes a backup file and puts each case back exactly as it was,
via `PUT /cases/{caseId}` — not the follow-up endpoint, so merge logic never
runs. Use `--dry-run` to see what it would do first:

```bash
make restore FILE=backups/cases-20260621T115111Z-LfgkHWYu.json DRY_RUN=1
make restore FILE=backups/cases-20260621T115111Z-LfgkHWYu.json
```

Restoring is idempotent: `PUT` always sets the case to exactly the state in
the file, so restoring the same file twice leaves the case unchanged rather
than compounding anything.

### Why `PUT /cases/{caseId}` exists separately from `POST /follow-ups`

These two write paths look similar but do very different things:

| Endpoint | Purpose | Runs merge logic? |
|---|---|---|
| `POST /cases/{caseId}/follow-ups` | Normal application flow — apply a new follow-up report to a case | Yes — computes `NEW`/`OVERRIDDEN`/`UNCHANGED`/`RETAINED` per field |
| `PUT /cases/{caseId}` | **Ops only**, used by `restore.sh` to put a case back exactly as a backup recorded it | No — raw overwrite, verbatim |

If `restore.sh` used the follow-up endpoint instead, the restored snapshot
would get merged against whatever the live case currently looks like, which
could change field statuses and values instead of recreating the backed-up
state exactly. `PUT` exists so a restore is a true rollback, not another
merge — it isn't meant to be used as a general "update a case" endpoint.

`GET /cases` (list all cases) exists for the same reason: `backup.sh` needs
to discover every case id without already knowing them upfront.

message.content: Okay can we implement swagger here

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: Found a real correctness gap: the generated schemas show `caseId`/`fieldPath` in camelCase, but the actual wire format is snake_case (`case_id`, `field_path`) due to the global Jackson naming strategy. Springdoc's schema resolver doesn't pick that up by default — it needs to be told to use the Spring-managed `ObjectMapper`. Fixing this now.

content.content: The file /Users/ashitaairan/Documents/pv-case-review/backend/src/main/java/com/pvreview/config/OpenApiConfig.java has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

lines: + "into stored cases and tracks reviewer queries against individual fields.")
+    // Without this, swagger-core resolves schema property names from raw Java field
+    // names (camelCase) instead of the app's actual snake_case wire format, since it
+    // otherwise builds its own ObjectMapper instead of using the Spring-configured one.

## Running it

```bash
./gradlew bootRun
```

The app starts on `http://localhost:8080`. On startup it bootstraps one case,
**`PV-2026-0451`**, from `src/main/resources/data/case_v1.json`, so there's
something to query immediately — no seeding step required.

## Running the tests

```bash
./gradlew test
```

## The four main endpoints

### 1. Get a case

```bash
curl http://localhost:8080/cases/PV-2026-0451
```

```json
{
  "case_id": "PV-2026-0451",
  "version": 1,
  "case_classification": "non-significant",
  "extracted_at": "2026-04-08T09:14:00Z",

input.new_string:
The app starts on `http://localhost:8080`. On startup it bootstraps one case,
**`PV-2026-0451`**, from `src/main/resources/data/case_v1.json`, so there's
something to query immediately — no seeding step required.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# PV Case Review — Backend

Spring Boot service for reviewing pharmacovigilance (PV) cases and merging in
follow-up reports. No database — cases and reviewer queries live in memory
for the lifetime of the process.

## Stack

- Java 17, Spring Boot 3.3, Gradle (wrapper included)
- Lombok for model/DTO boilerplate
- JSON request/response bodies use `snake_case` everywhere (`case_id`,
  `field_path`, etc.) via a global Jackson naming strategy — fields that are
  `null` are omitted from responses rather than serialized as `null`

## Running it

```bash
./gradlew bootRun
```

The app starts on `http://localhost:8080`. On startup it bootstraps one case,
**`PV-2026-0451`**, from `src/main/resources/data/case_v1.json`, so there's
something to query immediately — no seeding step required.

## Running the tests

```bash
./gradlew test
```

## The four main endpoints

### 1. Get a case

```bash
curl http://localhost:8080/cases/PV-2026-0451
```

```json
{
  "case_id": "PV-2026-0451",
  "version": 1,
  "case_classification": "non-significant",
  "extracted_at": "2026-04-08T09:14:00Z",
  "source_document": "initial_report_PV-2026-0451.pdf",
  "sections": {
    "patient": {
      "age": { "value": "62", "confidence": 0.91, "source": "p.2 §1" },
      "weight_kg": { "value": "78", "confidence": 0.85, "source": "p.3 §2" }
    }
  },
  "missing_fields": []
}
```

Unknown case id → `404` with an `ErrorResponse` body.

### 2. Apply a follow-up report (merge)

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/cases/PV-2026-0451/follow-ups \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "case_classification": "significant",
    "sections": {
      "patient": {
        "age": { "value": "63", "confidence": 0.90, "source": "p.2 §1" }
      },
      "adverse_event": {
        "severity_grade": { "value": "Grade 2", "confidence": 0.83, "source": "p.4 §3" }
      },
      "lab_results": {
        "ck_level": { "value": "450 U/L", "confidence": 0.89, "source": "p.6 §1" }
      }
    },
    "missing_fields": ["patient.weight_kg"]
  }'
```

```json
{
  "case_id": "PV-2026-0451",
  "version": 2,
  "case_classification": "significant",
  "sections": {
    "patient": {
      "age": { "value": "63", "confidence": 0.90, "source": "p.2 §1", "status": "OVERRIDDEN", "previous_value": "62" },
      "weight_kg": { "value": "78", "confidence": 0.85, "source": "p.3 §2", "status": "RETAINED" }
    },
    "adverse_event": {
      "severity_grade": { "value": "Grade 2", "confidence": 0.83, "source": "p.4 §3", "status": "NEW" }
    },
    "lab_results": {
      "ck_level": { "value": "450 U/L", "confidence": 0.89, "source": "p.6 §1", "status": "NEW" }
    }
  },
  "missing_fields": ["patient.weight_kg"]
}
```

`version` auto-increments if you don't supply one. Unknown case id → `404`;
malformed JSON body → `400`.

### 3. Submit a reviewer query against a field

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/queries \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "case_id": "PV-2026-0451",
    "field_path": "patient.age",
    "question": "Why did age change from 62 to 63?"
  }'
```

```json
{
  "id": "6f9ee839-db97-49e4-90e6-260761a210c1",
  "case_id": "PV-2026-0451",
  "field_path": "patient.age",
  "question": "Why did age change from 62 to 63?",
  "created_at": "2026-06-21T10:04:53.312608Z"
}
```

Any blank field → `400` (e.g. `"question is required"`).

### 4. List queries for a case

```bash
curl "http://localhost:8080/queries?caseId=PV-2026-0451"
```

```json
[
  {
    "id": "6f9ee839-db97-49e4-90e6-260761a210c1",
    "case_id": "PV-2026-0451",
    "field_path": "patient.age",
    "question": "Why did age change from 62 to 63?",
    "created_at": "2026-06-21T10:04:53.312608Z"
  }
]
```

Note: `caseId` here is a URL query parameter, not a JSON body field, so it
stays camelCase — the `snake_case` rule only applies to JSON bodies.

*(There's also a `GET /health` liveness check, returning `{"status": "UP"}`.)*

## Merge behavior

Every field on a case carries a `status` describing how it got its current
value, computed each time a follow-up is applied:

| Status | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `NEW` | Field (or whole section) didn't exist before; the follow-up introduced it. |
| `OVERRIDDEN` | Field existed before with a different value; the follow-up replaced it. `previous_value` holds what it used to be. |
| `UNCHANGED` | Field existed before with the *same* value; the follow-up just re-confirmed it. |
| `RETAINED` | Field existed before and the follow-up **didn't mention it at all**; the stored value carries forward as-is. |

### Why `RETAINED` exists

A follow-up report only contains the fields the extractor found evidence for
in *that* document — it's a partial payload, not a full restatement of the
case. If a field is missing from the follow-up, that's almost always because
the new document simply didn't re-cover it, not because it should be erased.
Treating "absent from follow-up" the same as "explicitly removed" would
silently destroy data from earlier reports, which is the wrong default for
PV case data where every value is sourced and audited.

So instead of overwriting or dropping anything, the merge keeps the stored
field's `value`/`confidence`/`source` untouched and tags it `RETAINED`,
making it visible to a reviewer as "carried forward, not reconfirmed" rather
than indistinguishable from a freshly-extracted field. This applies at both
granularities: a single field can be retained inside an otherwise-updated
section (e.g. `patient.weight_kg` above), and a whole section omitted from
the follow-up is retained field-by-field, not dropped wholesale.

## Error responses

All errors share one shape:

```json
{
  "status": 404,
  "error": "Not Found",
  "message": "No case found with id UNKNOWN-ID",
  "path": "/cases/UNKNOWN-ID",
  "timestamp": "2026-06-21T10:07:46.857813Z"
}
```

| Situation | Status |
|---|---|
| Unknown `caseId` | `404` |
| Malformed JSON body | `400` |
| Missing/blank required field (e.g. in `/queries`) | `400` |
| Missing required query param (e.g. `caseId` on `GET /queries`) | `400` |

## Operations

Running via Docker, plus backup/restore tooling under `ops/`. None of this
needs the JVM or Gradle on the host — only Docker.

`Makefile`, `Dockerfile`, `docker-compose.yml`, and `ops/` all live at the
**repository root** (one level up from this `backend/` directory, which
holds only the Spring Boot application itself) — run every command below
from there.

### Running via Docker

```bash
docker compose up -d --build
```

Builds the image (multi-stage: JDK to compile, JRE to run, non-root user)
and starts the backend on `http://localhost:8080` — same app as
`./gradlew bootRun`, just containerized. `docker-compose.yml` sets
`restart: "no"` on purpose: `CaseStore` is in-memory, so an auto-restarting
container would come back up with an empty store, silently losing whatever
was there before the crash.

### `ops/run.sh` and the Makefile

`ops/run.sh` wraps Docker Compose and Gradle with the usual lifecycle
subcommands; the `Makefile` just calls it so you can use whichever you
prefer:

```bash
./ops/run.sh build   /  make build    # docker compose build
./ops/run.sh start   /  make start    # docker compose up -d
./ops/run.sh stop    /  make stop     # docker compose down
./ops/run.sh test    /  make test     # ./gradlew test (doesn't need Docker)
./ops/run.sh logs    /  make logs     # follow container logs
./ops/run.sh clean   /  make clean    # stop containers, remove the image, ./gradlew clean
./ops/run.sh --help  /  make help
```

Every subcommand except `test` and `--help` checks `docker info` first and
fails with a one-line error if Docker isn't running, instead of letting a
raw Docker error leak through.

### Backups and restores

`backup.sh` fetches every case from a running backend and writes them to a
single timestamped, gitignored file:

```bash
make backup
# [2026-06-21T11:51:11Z] Starting backup from http://localhost:8080/cases
# [2026-06-21T11:51:11Z] Backup complete: 1 case(s) written to backups/cases-20260621T115111Z-LfgkHWYu.json
```

`restore.sh` takes a backup file and puts each case back exactly as it was,
via `PUT /cases/{caseId}` — not the follow-up endpoint, so merge logic never
runs. Use `--dry-run` to see what it would do first:

```bash
make restore FILE=backups/cases-20260621T115111Z-LfgkHWYu.json DRY_RUN=1
make restore FILE=backups/cases-20260621T115111Z-LfgkHWYu.json
```

Restoring is idempotent: `PUT` always sets the case to exactly the state in
the file, so restoring the same file twice leaves the case unchanged rather
than compounding anything.

### Why `PUT /cases/{caseId}` exists separately from `POST /follow-ups`

These two write paths look similar but do very different things:

| Endpoint | Purpose | Runs merge logic? |
|---|---|---|
| `POST /cases/{caseId}/follow-ups` | Normal application flow — apply a new follow-up report to a case | Yes — computes `NEW`/`OVERRIDDEN`/`UNCHANGED`/`RETAINED` per field |
| `PUT /cases/{caseId}` | **Ops only**, used by `restore.sh` to put a case back exactly as a backup recorded it | No — raw overwrite, verbatim |

If `restore.sh` used the follow-up endpoint instead, the restored snapshot
would get merged against whatever the live case currently looks like, which
could change field statuses and values instead of recreating the backed-up
state exactly. `PUT` exists so a restore is a true rollback, not another
merge — it isn't meant to be used as a general "update a case" endpoint.

`GET /cases` (list all cases) exists for the same reason: `backup.sh` needs
to discover every case id without already knowing them upfront.

content.text:
Swagger is implemented and verified:

- Added `springdoc-openapi-starter-webmvc-ui:2.6.0` to `build.gradle`
- Added `com.pvreview.config.OpenApiConfig` with API title/description/version, plus a `ModelResolver` bean wired to the Spring-managed `ObjectMapper`
- **Caught and fixed a real bug along the way**: springdoc's default schema generation ignored the app's global `SNAKE_CASE` Jackson naming strategy, so generated schemas showed `caseId`/`fieldPath` instead of the actual wire format `case_id`/`field_path`. Explicitly wiring the `ModelResolver` to the app's `ObjectMapper` fixed it — verified `Case`, `FieldRecord`, `QueryRequest` schemas all now show correct snake_case fields, including the `MergeStatus` enum values on `status`
- `/swagger-ui/index.html` and `/v3/api-docs` both verified reachable (HTTP 200)
- Full test suite (21 tests) still passes
- Added a one-line pointer to the Swagger URLs in `backend/README.md`

Nothing has been committed — these changes are sitting in the working tree, ready for you to review.

What this error is Responses Curl curl -X 'GET' \ 'http://localhost:8080/cases/http%3A%2F%2Flocalhost%3A8080%2Fswagger-ui%2Findex.html' \ -H 'accept: */*' Request URL http://localhost:8080/cases/http%3A%2F%2Flocalhost%3A8080%2Fswagger-ui%2Findex.html Server response Code Details 400 Undocumented Error: response status is 400 Response body Download HTTP Status 400 – Bad Request

HTTP Status 400 – Bad Request

Response headers connection: close content-language: en content-length: 435 content-type: text/html;charset=utf-8 date: Sun,21 Jun 2026 18:56:49 GMT Responses Code Description When I am using swaggger I am getting this error

content.text:
That's not an app bug — you tested the endpoint with the wrong value. Here's what happened:

The `GET /cases/{caseId}` request's `caseId` path parameter got filled with the literal string `http://localhost:8080/swagger-ui/index.html` (URL-encoded as `http%3A%2F%2F...`) instead of an actual case ID. So the request became:

```
GET /cases/http%3A%2F%2Flocalhost%3A8080%2Fswagger-ui%2Findex.html
```

Tomcat decodes `%2F` back into `/` characters, and by default it **rejects encoded slashes inside a URL path segment** as a security precaution (it's a classic path-traversal/filter-bypass vector — letting `%2F` masquerade as extra path segments). So the request never even reaches Spring's `DispatcherServlet`, my `CaseController`, or `GlobalExceptionHandler` — Tomcat's connector blocks it at the very front door. That's why the response body is the raw default Tomcat error page (`<title>HTTP Status 400 – Bad Request</title>`) instead of the app's normal JSON error shape (`{"status":404,"error":"Not Found",...}`) you'd see for a genuinely unknown case ID.

**Fix:** in Swagger UI's "Try it out" form, put an actual case ID in the `caseId` field — e.g. `PV-2026-0451` — not a URL. Then it'll correctly return the case (or a proper `404` JSON if the ID doesn't exist).

This is just a one-off usage mistake, not something that needs a code change — unless you specifically want malformed/encoded paths like this to also return your app's consistent `ErrorResponse` JSON instead of Tomcat's raw HTML page. Want me to add that?

content.text:
Yes — let me trace through the code, then prove it live. Looking at `MergeService.mergeFields()` (`backend/src/main/java/com/pvreview/service/MergeService.java:79-91`) and `mergeField()` (line 93-120):

- **New field in an existing section**: `mergeFields` iterates over the *union* of payload field keys and current field keys (`orderedKeys`, line 82). For a key that only exists in the payload, `oldField` is `null`, so `mergeField(null, newField)` skips the "both present" branch and the "old only" branch, falling to the `newField != null` branch → tagged `NEW`.
- **Brand new section**: `mergeSections` (line 62-77) does the same union over section keys. For a section that doesn't exist yet in the stored case, `currentFields` resolves to an empty map (`getOrDefault(sectionKey, Collections.emptyMap())`, line 72). Every field in that new section then hits the same "old only is empty" path as above → every field in the new section gets `NEW`. There's no separate "section status" — sections aren't tracked as a unit, only their individual fields are, so a new section is just "all its fields are NEW."

Let me prove this live rather than just read the code.

message.content:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   This session continued a multi-phase Spring Boot + ops tooling + React frontend project for a pharmacovigilance (PV) case review platform (case ID `PV-2026-0451`). The user's explicit sequential requests in this session were:
   - Functionally verify `backend/ops/run.sh` (build/start/stop/test/logs/clean/--help, graceful Docker-down failure) before moving on — this was the carry-over task from before the summary.
   - "Now backup.sh — the brief says it should fetch all cases from the running service, write to a timestamped file under backups/, log to stderr with timestamps, and be safe to cron. What do you need from me before you write it?" — explicitly inviting clarifying questions.
   - "Now restore.sh. It should take a backup file as argument, PUT each case back using PUT /cases/{caseId}, and support a --dry-run flag that shows what would happen without making changes. Should be idempotent."
   - "Now we need the Makefile and the Operations README section."
   - "Hey the Makefile, Dockerfile, docker-compose.yml and ops/ folder all got created inside backend/ but they should be at the repo root level. The repo structure should be: [tree diagram with backend/, ops/, Makefile, Dockerfile, docker-compose.yml all siblings at root]. Can you move everything to the right place?"
   - "Can you scaffold an empty Vite + React project in the frontend/ folder? Just the toolchain setup, no actual components yet. I need it ready for the live session."
   - "Okay can we implement swagger here" (most recent, currently in progress).

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - Spring Boot 3.3.4, Java 17, Gradle wrapper, Lombok
   - In-memory `ConcurrentHashMap`-backed `CaseStore` (no persistence) — drives the `restart: "no"` Docker Compose decision and the ops-only `PUT /cases/{caseId}` design
   - Global Jackson `SNAKE_CASE` naming strategy (`spring.jackson.property-naming-strategy: SNAKE_CASE`) + `default-property-inclusion: non_null` — critical context for the Swagger bug found this session
   - Merge semantics: `MergeStatus` enum (`NEW`, `OVERRIDDEN`, `UNCHANGED`, `RETAINED`)
   - REST design split: `POST /cases/{caseId}/follow-ups` (business merge) vs `PUT /cases/{caseId}` (ops-only raw overwrite, used only by `restore.sh`) vs `GET /cases` (list, used only by `backup.sh`)
   - Docker multi-stage builds (`eclipse-temurin:17-jdk-jammy` build stage / `17-jre-jammy` runtime stage), non-root container user, `HEALTHCHECK`, Docker Compose `restart: "no"` policy reasoning
   - Bash scripting conventions: `set -euo pipefail`, `BASH_SOURCE`-based path resolution, `mktemp` for atomic/unique temp files, curl response/status-code splitting via `-w \n%{http_code}'` + parameter expansion (`${response##*\n'}` / `${response%\n'*}`), graceful `command -v`-based dependency checks, `require_docker()` pre-flight pattern
   - Git: `git mv` for rename-history-preserving file moves; repo already tracked with prior commits and an `origin/main` remote; never commit without explicit user ask (honored — moves/additions left staged/unstaged, not committed)
   - Vite + React + TypeScript scaffolding (`npm create vite@latest <dir> -- --template react-ts`), npm scripts (`dev`, `build` = `tsc -b && vite build`, `lint`, `preview`)
   - springdoc-openapi (`springdoc-openapi-starter-webmvc-ui:2.6.0`) for Spring Boot 3 OpenAPI/Swagger UI generation
   - swagger-core `ModelResolver`/`ModelConverter` and the gotcha that it builds its own default `ObjectMapper` unless explicitly wired to the Spring-managed one — required to make generated schemas match the app's actual snake_case wire format

3. Files and Code Sections:

   - **`backend/ops/run.sh`** (later moved to `ops/run.sh` at repo root)
     - Verified all subcommands functionally; later edited during the file-move task. Final relevant section after the move:
       ```bash
       SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
       ROOT_DIR="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"
       BACKEND_DIR="$ROOT_DIR/backend"
       ```
       and each `cmd_*` function updated so `cmd_build`/`cmd_start`/`cmd_stop`/`cmd_logs`/`cmd_clean`'s docker-compose calls `cd "$ROOT_DIR"`, while `cmd_test` and the `./gradlew clean` line inside `cmd_clean` `cd "$BACKEND_DIR"`.

   - **`backend/ops/backup.sh`** (later moved to `ops/backup.sh` at repo root)
     - Created fresh this session. Key design: `BASE_URL="${PV_BACKEND_URL:-http://localhost:8080}"`; `log()` helper timestamps to stderr; `require_cmd` checks for `curl`/`jq`; writes to a `mktemp`-generated temp file first, validates HTTP 200 + valid JSON via `jq empty`, then renames into place.
     - **Bug found and fixed**: original version derived `dest` filename from `timestamp` alone, causing same-second collisions to silently overwrite via `mv`. Fixed by extracting the unique suffix from the temp file's own mktemp-generated name:
       ```bash
       timestamp="$(date -u +%Y%m%dT%H%M%SZ)"
       tmp_file="$(mktemp "$BACKUP_DIR/.cases-tmp.XXXXXXXX")"
       unique="${tmp_file##*.cases-tmp.}"
       dest="$BACKUP_DIR/cases-${timestamp}-${unique}.json"
       ```
     - After the repo-root move, path logic became:
       ```bash
       SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
       ROOT_DIR="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"
       BACKUP_DIR="$ROOT_DIR/backups"
       ```

   - **`backend/ops/restore.sh`** (later moved to `ops/restore.sh` at repo root)
     - Created fresh this session, no path-resolution logic at all (operates purely on `BASE_URL` env var and a positional backup-file argument), so required zero edits during the file move.
     - Manual arg parsing loop recognizing `--dry-run`, `--help`/`-h`, unknown `-*` options, and one positional `BACKUP_FILE`.
     - Validates: file exists, is valid JSON (`jq empty`), is a JSON array (`jq -r 'type'`).
     - Per-case loop via `while IFS= read -r case_json; do ... done < <(jq -c '.[]' "$BACKUP_FILE")` (process substitution to avoid subshell variable loss).
     - PUT + status-code split pattern:
       ```bash
       response="$(curl -sS -X PUT -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
         --data-binary "$case_json" -w \n%{http_code}' "$BASE_URL/cases/$case_id" || true)"
       http_code="${response##*\n'}"
       body="${response%\n'*}"
       ```
     - Idempotency by construction: `PUT /cases/{caseId}` is always a raw overwrite (no merge), so restoring the same file twice yields identical state — verified live.

   - **`backend/Makefile`** (later moved to `Makefile` at repo root) — unchanged in content during the move (still references `./ops/run.sh`, `./ops/backup.sh`, `./ops/restore.sh`, valid since `ops/` is now a root sibling). Targets: `help` (default, first target), `build`, `start`, `stop`, `test`, `logs`, `clean`, `backup`, `restore` (`FILE=` and `DRY_RUN=` variables: `./ops/restore.sh $(if $(DRY_RUN),--dry-run) $(FILE)`).

   - **`backend/Dockerfile`** (later moved to `Dockerfile` at repo root)
     - COPY paths updated for the new root build context:
       ```dockerfile
       COPY backend/gradlew backend/settings.gradle backend/build.gradle ./
       COPY backend/gradle ./gradle
       RUN ./gradlew dependencies --no-daemon

       COPY backend/src ./src
       RUN ./gradlew bootJar --no-daemon
       ```
       Runtime stage (JRE, non-root user, HEALTHCHECK) unchanged.

   - **`backend/.dockerignore`** (later moved to `.dockerignore` at repo root)
     - Rewritten for the new root context:
       ```
       .git/
       backend/build/
       backend/.gradle/
       backend/out/
       backend/*.log
       **/.idea/
       **/*.iml
       backups/
       ```

   - **`backend/docker-compose.yml`** (later moved to `docker-compose.yml` at repo root) — content unchanged (`build: .` correctly resolves relative to wherever the compose file lives).

   - **`backend/README.md`**
     - Added "## Operations" section (Docker usage, `restart: "no"` rationale, `ops/run.sh`+Makefile command table, backup/restore usage + idempotency note, table explaining `PUT /cases/{caseId}` vs `POST /follow-ups`, `GET /cases` rationale).
     - After the file move, added a clarifying note directly under the Operations heading: "`Makefile`, `Dockerfile`, `docker-compose.yml`, and `ops/` all live at the **repository root** (one level up from this `backend/` directory, which holds only the Spring Boot application itself) — run every command below from there."
     - Most recent edit (Swagger task): added to the "Running it" section, right after the bootstrap-case paragraph:
       ```
       Interactive API docs (Swagger UI) are at `http://localhost:8080/swagger-ui/index.html`,
       backed by the raw OpenAPI spec at `http://localhost:8080/v3/api-docs`.
       ```

   - **`.gitignore`** (repo root) — confirmed already contains `backups/` (pre-existing, unanchored pattern matches at any depth, so it covers both old `backend/backups/` and new root `backups/` without modification) and a `# Node (frontend, Phase 2)` section with `node_modules/`, `.next/`, `dist/` (pre-existing, confirmed it covers `frontend/node_modules/` and `frontend/dist/` without modification).

   - **`frontend/`** (new, created this session)
     - Scaffolded via `npm create vite@latest frontend -- --template react-ts`. Standard Vite React-TS template: `package.json` (react ^19.2.6, react-dom ^19.2.6, vite ^8.0.12, typescript ~6.0.2, eslint ^10.3.0, etc.), `vite.config.ts`, `tsconfig.json`/`tsconfig.app.json`/`tsconfig.node.json`, `eslint.config.js`, `src/App.tsx`, `src/main.tsx`, `index.html`, its own `.gitignore`.

No components added — explicitly per user's "Just the toolchain setup, no actual components yet" instruction.        package com.pvreview.config;

       import io.swagger.v3.core.converter.ModelConverter;

       import io.swagger.v3.oas.models.OpenAPI;

file.content:
# PV Case Review — Backend

Spring Boot service for reviewing pharmacovigilance (PV) cases and merging in
follow-up reports. No database — cases and reviewer queries live in memory
for the lifetime of the process.

## Stack

- Java 17, Spring Boot 3.3, Gradle (wrapper included)
- Lombok for model/DTO boilerplate
- JSON request/response bodies use `snake_case` everywhere (`case_id`,
  `field_path`, etc.) via a global Jackson naming strategy — fields that are
  `null` are omitted from responses rather than serialized as `null`

## Running it

```bash
./gradlew bootRun
```

The app starts on `http://localhost:8080`. On startup it bootstraps one case,
**`PV-2026-0451`**, from `src/main/resources/data/case_v1.json`, so there's
something to query immediately — no seeding step required.

Interactive API docs (Swagger UI) are at `http://localhost:8080/swagger-ui/index.html`,
backed by the raw OpenAPI spec at `http://localhost:8080/v3/api-docs`.

## Running the tests

```bash
./gradlew test
```

## The four main endpoints

### 1. Get a case

```bash
curl http://localhost:8080/cases/PV-2026-0451
```

```json
{
  "case_id": "PV-2026-0451",
  "version": 1,
  "case_classification": "non-significant",
  "extracted_at": "2026-04-08T09:14:00Z",
  "source_document": "initial_report_PV-2026-0451.pdf",
  "sections": {
    "patient": {
      "age": { "value": "62", "confidence": 0.91, "source": "p.2 §1" },
      "weight_kg": { "value": "78", "confidence": 0.85, "source": "p.3 §2" }
    }
  },
  "missing_fields": []
}
```

Unknown case id → `404` with an `ErrorResponse` body.

### 2. Apply a follow-up report (merge)

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/cases/PV-2026-0451/follow-ups \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "case_classification": "significant",
    "sections": {
      "patient": {
        "age": { "value": "63", "confidence": 0.90, "source": "p.2 §1" }
      },
      "adverse_event": {
        "severity_grade": { "value": "Grade 2", "confidence": 0.83, "source": "p.4 §3" }
      },
      "lab_results": {
        "ck_level": { "value": "450 U/L", "confidence": 0.89, "source": "p.6 §1" }
      }
    },
    "missing_fields": ["patient.weight_kg"]
  }'
```

```json
{
  "case_id": "PV-2026-0451",
  "version": 2,
  "case_classification": "significant",
  "sections": {
    "patient": {
      "age": { "value": "63", "confidence": 0.90, "source": "p.2 §1", "status": "OVERRIDDEN", "previous_value": "62" },
      "weight_kg": { "value": "78", "confidence": 0.85, "source": "p.3 §2", "status": "RETAINED" }
    },
    "adverse_event": {
      "severity_grade": { "value": "Grade 2", "confidence": 0.83, "source": "p.4 §3", "status": "NEW" }
    },
    "lab_results": {
      "ck_level": { "value": "450 U/L", "confidence": 0.89, "source": "p.6 §1", "status": "NEW" }
    }
  },
  "missing_fields": ["patient.weight_kg"]
}
```

`version` auto-increments if you don't supply one. Unknown case id → `404`;
malformed JSON body → `400`.

### 3. Submit a reviewer query against a field

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/queries \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "case_id": "PV-2026-0451",
    "field_path": "patient.age",
    "question": "Why did age change from 62 to 63?"
  }'
```

```json
{
  "id": "6f9ee839-db97-49e4-90e6-260761a210c1",
  "case_id": "PV-2026-0451",
  "field_path": "patient.age",
  "question": "Why did age change from 62 to 63?",
  "created_at": "2026-06-21T10:04:53.312608Z"
}
```

Any blank field → `400` (e.g. `"question is required"`).

### 4. List queries for a case

```bash
curl "http://localhost:8080/queries?caseId=PV-2026-0451"
```

```json
[
  {
    "id": "6f9ee839-db97-49e4-90e6-260761a210c1",
    "case_id": "PV-2026-0451",
    "field_path": "patient.age",
    "question": "Why did age change from 62 to 63?",
    "created_at": "2026-06-21T10:04:53.312608Z"
  }
]
```

Note: `caseId` here is a URL query parameter, not a JSON body field, so it
stays camelCase — the `snake_case` rule only applies to JSON bodies.

*(There's also a `GET /health` liveness check, returning `{"status": "UP"}`.)*

## Merge behavior

Every field on a case carries a `status` describing how it got its current
value, computed each time a follow-up is applied:

| Status | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `NEW` | Field (or whole section) didn't exist before; the follow-up introduced it. |
| `OVERRIDDEN` | Field existed before with a different value; the follow-up replaced it. `previous_value` holds what it used to be. |
| `UNCHANGED` | Field existed before with the *same* value; the follow-up just re-confirmed it. |
| `RETAINED` | Field existed before and the follow-up **didn't mention it at all**; the stored value carries forward as-is. |

### Why `RETAINED` exists

A follow-up report only contains the fields the extractor found evidence for
in *that* document — it's a partial payload, not a full restatement of the
case. If a field is missing from the follow-up, that's almost always because
the new document simply didn't re-cover it, not because it should be erased.
Treating "absent from follow-up" the same as "explicitly removed" would
silently destroy data from earlier reports, which is the wrong default for
PV case data where every value is sourced and audited.

So instead of overwriting or dropping anything, the merge keeps the stored
field's `value`/`confidence`/`source` untouched and tags it `RETAINED`,
making it visible to a reviewer as "carried forward, not reconfirmed" rather
than indistinguishable from a freshly-extracted field. This applies at both
granularities: a single field can be retained inside an otherwise-updated
section (e.g. `patient.weight_kg` above), and a whole section omitted from
the follow-up is retained field-by-field, not dropped wholesale.

## Error responses

All errors share one shape:

```json
{
  "status": 404,
  "error": "Not Found",
  "message": "No case found with id UNKNOWN-ID",
  "path": "/cases/UNKNOWN-ID",
  "timestamp": "2026-06-21T10:07:46.857813Z"
}
```

| Situation | Status |
|---|---|
| Unknown `caseId` | `404` |
| Malformed JSON body | `400` |
| Missing/blank required field (e.g. in `/queries`) | `400` |
| Missing required query param (e.g. `caseId` on `GET /queries`) | `400` |

## Operations

Running via Docker, plus backup/restore tooling under `ops/`. None of this
needs the JVM or Gradle on the host — only Docker.

`Makefile`, `Dockerfile`, `docker-compose.yml`, and `ops/` all live at the
**repository root** (one level up from this `backend/` directory, which
holds only the Spring Boot application itself) — run every command below
from there.

### Running via Docker

```bash
docker compose up -d --build
```

Builds the image (multi-stage: JDK to compile, JRE to run, non-root user)
and starts the backend on `http://localhost:8080` — same app as
`./gradlew bootRun`, just containerized. `docker-compose.yml` sets
`restart: "no"` on purpose: `CaseStore` is in-memory, so an auto-restarting
container would come back up with an empty store, silently losing whatever
was there before the crash.

### `ops/run.sh` and the Makefile

`ops/run.sh` wraps Docker Compose and Gradle with the usual lifecycle
subcommands; the `Makefile` just calls it so you can use whichever you
prefer:

```bash
./ops/run.sh build   /  make build    # docker compose build
./ops/run.sh start   /  make start    # docker compose up -d
./ops/run.sh stop    /  make stop     # docker compose down
./ops/run.sh test    /  make test     # ./gradlew test (doesn't need Docker)
./ops/run.sh logs    /  make logs     # follow container logs
./ops/run.sh clean   /  make clean    # stop containers, remove the image, ./gradlew clean
./ops/run.sh --help  /  make help
```

Every subcommand except `test` and `--help` checks `docker info` first and
fails with a one-line error if Docker isn't running, instead of letting a
raw Docker error leak through.

### Backups and restores

`backup.sh` fetches every case from a running backend and writes them to a
single timestamped, gitignored file:

```bash
make backup
# [2026-06-21T11:51:11Z] Starting backup from http://localhost:8080/cases
# [2026-06-21T11:51:11Z] Backup complete: 1 case(s) written to backups/cases-20260621T115111Z-LfgkHWYu.json
```

`restore.sh` takes a backup file and puts each case back exactly as it was,
via `PUT /cases/{caseId}` — not the follow-up endpoint, so merge logic never
runs. Use `--dry-run` to see what it would do first:

```bash
make restore FILE=backups/cases-20260621T115111Z-LfgkHWYu.json DRY_RUN=1
make restore FILE=backups/cases-20260621T115111Z-LfgkHWYu.json
```

Restoring is idempotent: `PUT` always sets the case to exactly the state in
the file, so restoring the same file twice leaves the case unchanged rather
than compounding anything.

### Why `PUT /cases/{caseId}` exists separately from `POST /follow-ups`

These two write paths look similar but do very different things:

| Endpoint | Purpose | Runs merge logic? |
|---|---|---|
| `POST /cases/{caseId}/follow-ups` | Normal application flow — apply a new follow-up report to a case | Yes — computes `NEW`/`OVERRIDDEN`/`UNCHANGED`/`RETAINED` per field |
| `PUT /cases/{caseId}` | **Ops only**, used by `restore.sh` to put a case back exactly as a backup recorded it | No — raw overwrite, verbatim |

If `restore.sh` used the follow-up endpoint instead, the restored snapshot
would get merged against whatever the live case currently looks like, which
could change field statuses and values instead of recreating the backed-up
state exactly. `PUT` exists so a restore is a true rollback, not another
merge — it isn't meant to be used as a general "update a case" endpoint.

`GET /cases` (list all cases) exists for the same reason: `backup.sh` needs
to discover every case id without already knowing them upfront.

services:
  backend:
    build: .
    image: pv-case-review-backend:local
    ports:
      - "8080:8080"
    restart: "no"
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "wget", "--no-verbose", "--tries=1", "--spider", "http://localhost:8080/health"]
      interval: 30s
      timeout: 3s
      start_period: 20s
      retries: 3

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

lastPrompt: what if new field or section came . Will it handling all these cases

content.text:
Confirmed live, exactly as the code predicts:

- **New field in an existing section** (`patient.blood_pressure`, didn't exist before) → `"status": "NEW"`, while the rest of `patient`'s pre-existing fields correctly stayed `RETAINED` (since this follow-up didn't restate them).
- **Brand new section** (`lab_results`, didn't exist at all in the stored case) → both its fields (`ck_level`, `alt_level`) came back `NEW`.

So to directly answer: yes, both cases are handled, and by the *same* code path — no special-casing needed. `mergeSections` and `mergeFields` both union the payload's keys with the current case's keys (`MergeService.java:71,82`), so a key that's only in the payload always has `oldField == null` on the current side, which falls into `mergeField`'s "new" branch regardless of whether that key is a field inside a known section or part of a section that doesn't exist yet. A "new section" isn't a distinct concept in the model — it's just a section where *every* field happens to be new, since `currentSections.getOrDefault(sectionKey, Collections.emptyMap())` (line 72) silently gives it an empty starting point.

content.text:
The error message is misleading — "Failed to fetch" with CORS/network boilerplate is **not** what's actually wrong. The real problem is in the request body you pasted: it's not valid JSON. Look at the `suspect_drug` section:

```json
"indication": {
  "value": "Hypercholesterolemia",
  "confidence": 0.88,
  "source": "p.3 §4"
}
"frequency": {        ← missing comma right above this line
  "value": "2",
  ...
}
```

There's no comma between the closing `}` of `indication` and the start of `frequency`. That's a JSON syntax error.

Swagger UI's body editor tries to parse the JSON client-side, in your browser, before it ever constructs the network request. When that parse throws, the request never leaves your browser — it never reaches the Spring Boot backend at all. Swagger UI's generic catch-all error handler then dumps that boilerplate "Failed to fetch / Possible Reasons: CORS, Network Failure, URL scheme" message regardless of the actual cause, which is why it looks like a network/CORS problem when it's really just a malformed-JSON problem in the textarea.

Let me prove this is purely a client-side parsing issue, not a backend one, by sending the exact same broken JSON straight to the server with curl.

input.description: Read CasePayload and RawField DTOs to compare field names

content.text:
Yes, the JSON is now correct on both counts:

**1. Syntax** — valid JSON (verified with `jq empty`), the missing comma after `indication` is fixed.

**2. Shape matches the API contract** — every key lines up with `CasePayload`/`RawField` (Jackson's global `SNAKE_CASE` strategy maps these straight to the Java fields):

| Your JSON key | Maps to |
|---|---|
| `case_id`, `version`, `case_classification`, `extracted_at`, `source_document`, `missing_fields` | `CasePayload` fields |
| `sections.<section>.<field>.{value, confidence, source}` | `RawField` |

This will be accepted by the endpoint. One thing worth knowing, not an error, just so the result isn't surprising: this payload is a **full restatement** of the whole case (every field from the original case is present), not a partial follow-up. That means nothing will come back `RETAINED` — `RETAINED` only happens when a field is *absent* from the payload. Here you'll instead see:
- `age`: `OVERRIDDEN` (62 → 63)
- `frequency`: `NEW` (didn't exist before)
- everything else: `UNCHANGED` (same value resubmitted)

That's all valid, just a different merge outcome than a sparse delta would produce. Want me to run it against the live backend to confirm the actual response?
Let me check whether the backend is actually still running — this looks different from the earlier JSON-syntax issue.

content.text:
Found it — confirmed nothing is listening on port 8080 right now. I shut the dev server down earlier (after the new-field/new-section demo) and the attempt to restart it afterward was a command you rejected, so it never came back up.

That's the actual cause this time — it's different from the earlier issue. Last time, the JSON itself was malformed and the browser's own JSON parser rejected it before any network call was attempted. This time the JSON is valid (confirmed), so the browser proceeds to `fetch()` the URL — but there's no server on `localhost:8080` to connect to, so the TCP connection itself is refused. The browser's `fetch()` promise rejects, and Swagger UI shows that same generic "Failed to fetch / CORS / Network Failure / URL scheme" boilerplate regardless of root cause — which is why it looks identical to the last error even though the underlying problem is completely different (dead server vs. malformed body).

That's also why you can't see `NEW`/`OVERRIDDEN`/`UNCHANGED` — the request never reached `MergeService` at all, so no response (and no statuses) ever came back.

Want me to restart the backend (`./gradlew bootRun` from `backend/`) so you can re-execute the request in Swagger and see the actual merge result?
